(2) Follow-on cleanup should physically move same-name legacy bodies out of canonical `skills/` into an archive/docs lineage area or merge them into the `gad-*` canonical versions. (3) Skill authoring remains free to use GAD-specific structure, references, and parsing conventions, but the externally parsed contract stays minimal and portable: valid YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, optional status/provenance fields) plus a stable SKILL.md shape. (4) `gad-quick-skill` / `create-proto-skill` and `gad-skill-creator` remain distinct because they serve different lifecycle stages, but they should feed one canonical promoted skill body rather than create long-lived duplicate public names.

## gad-179: Context framework is a new catalog type parallel to skills/agents/workflows/tech-stacks â€” installable as a project dependency; GAD remains framed as a framework

### Summary

Confirms and extends decision gad-176. **Context framework** becomes a new catalog content type sitting alongside skills, agents, commands, workflows, and tech stacks in `catalog.generated.ts`. Each context framework is a bundle referencing existing skill/agent/workflow slugs by identifier â€” it does NOT duplicate their content. A project declares the context framework it uses as a dependency, the same way it declares a tech stack (kaplay, phaser, three) or a skill set; the Brood Editor can swap the declared framework on the fly and the site re-renders the project's "installed" skill/agent/workflow set based on the new framework's bundle. **Bundle-by-reference, not by copy** â€” if a context framework references `skill:gad-loop` and the GAD skill ships an update, every project on that framework picks up the update without re-bundling. A framework can extend another (e.g. a future "GAD-lite" bundle references most GAD skills but drops a few and adds none). **Framing**: GAD itself stays framed as a framework â€” the playground, marketplace, and brood editor are features layered on top, not a replacement for the framework identity. The site is not moving away from "GAD is a framework for getting anything done"; it's moving toward "GAD is a framework, and it happens to ship an interactive playground where you can try other frameworks and tech stacks alongside it." The framework comparison matrix (gad-164) is the canonical list of first-party context frameworks: bare, GSD, GAD, custom.

### Impact

(1) Phase 42.4 goal updated: `ContextFramework` is a new top-level catalog type in catalog.generated.ts (interface: id, name, description, version, skills[], agents[], workflows[], canonicalProjects[], extends?). build-site-data gains a `parseContextFrameworks()` extractor reading `.planning/context-frameworks/*.md`. The first three entries are `bare.md`, `gsd.md`, `gad.md`. (2) Project metadata (project.yml / gad.json / species schema) gains a `context_framework: <slug>` field. Phase 43's species schema unification needs to add it. (3) Project Market marketplace (phase 44) gains a faceted filter chip for context framework, parallel to tech-stack and domain filters. (4) Brood Editor (phase 44.5) gains a "swap context framework" action in the project inspector. Swapping recomputes the project's effective skill/agent/workflow set based on the new framework bundle and re-renders. (5) Phase 45 rebrand copy keeps "GAD is a framework" as the primary frame. Secondary tagline: "with a playground for trying other frameworks, tech stacks, and domains side by side." (6) The existing SKILL_INHERITANCE table in catalog.generated.ts is a natural starting point for context framework bundling â€” it already records which framework skills appear in which eval project template.

## gad-178: Skills determine workflows â€” tool-level mining is a v1 shortcut; skill invocation sequences are the real signal

### Summary

A workflow is defined by the SKILLS used and the ORDER they're used in, not by the low-level tools those skills invoke. The v1 detector mines `tool_use` events (Bash / Read / Edit / Write / Grep etc.) because that is the only signal the current trace schema carries â€” and its top patterns are noise like `Bash â†’ Bash â†’ Bash` and `Read â†’ Edit â†’ Edit` which describe nothing about WHAT the agent was trying to accomplish, only the mechanics of how editing happens. The target signal is skill invocations: `check-todos â†’ task-checkpoint â†’ gad-execute-phase` means something; `Bash Ã— 271` does not. **Policy**: workflow identity is determined by the sequence of skill invocations plus the CLI commands and planning artifacts they touch. Tool-level events become a secondary stream used for noise filtering (events that don't belong to any skill) and for spotting gaps where a skill should exist but none does yet. **v2 detector** (task 42.3-16) mines `skill_use` events directly and groups tool-level activity under the skill context that produced it. **Workflow patterns are recognized from how skills are used over time** â€” temporal patterns, chain frequencies, skill co-occurrence, and skill-to-artifact correlation â€” not from instantaneous tool sequences. The user explicitly flagged this as "that is why I want to deep dive that at some point," so a deep-dive discuss round is queued before the detector v2 implementation.

### Impact

(1) Decision gad-173's explicit `workflow_enter` / `workflow_exit` events are no longer optional â€” they become the primary input for skill-level synthesis. Skills must emit these as structured trace events so the synthesizer has authoritative markers. Falls back to inferring skill identity from adjacent tool calls when markers are missing (legacy traces, non-GAD skills). (2) New task 42.3-16: detector v2 mines skill sequences as the primary signal and surfaces tool-level patterns only when they don't map to a known skill. (3) The v1 emergent candidates currently on /planning (`bash-bash-bash-271`, `read-edit-edit-26`, etc.) are labeled "v1 tool-level patterns" in the UI so readers don't mistake them for real workflows. (4) Tool-level candidates that persist after v2 filtering are genuine noise-or-gap signals: either a real pattern the skill catalog doesn't cover yet, or mechanical noise. Distinguishing these becomes a third classifier pass. (5) Hook-level scope tagging (task 42.3-14) must land before or alongside the detector v2 â€” workflow_enter/exit events are the scope mechanism for skill-level synthesis. (6) The deep-dive discuss round for 42.3-16 must answer: does a skill's trace boundary start at the Skill tool call or at the first tool_use after? How do we identify a skill invocation when it isn't wrapped in an explicit Skill tool call (e.g. a skill that just tells the agent what to do without a tool)? How do we handle nested skill invocations?

## gad-177: Emergent workflows have NO authored Mermaid â€” React Flow only; the live graph IS the workflow shape

### Summary

Authored workflows are designed in advance; Mermaid is the right tool for their expected shape (crisp, static, diffable). Emergent workflows are detected from trace data; by definition they have no designed shape to render as an expected Mermaid graph. The synthetic Mermaid body the v1 detector was generating (auto-constructed from the tool sequence) was conceptually misleading â€” it implied someone intended that shape, which is false. Policy: emergent workflows render as a single React Flow graph, no Mermaid pane, no expected/actual diff (the live graph IS the workflow). Authored workflows keep the two-pane Mermaid+React-Flow side-by-side layout. `WorkflowCard` branches on `origin` and uses different layouts: authored = two-pane grid with metadata header and participant badges; emergent = single-column compact card with minimal header.

### Impact

(1) `detectEmergentWorkflows()` no longer emits a `mermaidBody` (empty string); the emergent entry also has no `conformance` score (nothing to diff against). (2) `WorkflowCard` detects `workflow.origin === "emergent"` and renders a compact single-column layout. (3) A new `compact` prop on `WorkflowCard` and `WorkflowLiveDiagram` supports the grid of emergent cards on /planning â€” smaller height (h-56 vs h-[480]), no MiniMap, truncated description. (4) `PlanningWorkflowsTab` renders the emergent section as a `md:grid-cols-2 xl:grid-cols-3` grid instead of a vertical stack so 12 candidates fit in 4 rows instead of 12. (5) This aligns with the framing: Mermaid is for design intent; React Flow is for observed reality.

## gad-176: Context framework is a first-class axis, orthogonal to tech stack â€” GAD is one of many; site treats them symmetrically

### Summary

The site has been implicitly treating GAD as "the framework" and everything else (bare, GSD) as comparison conditions. That framing is wrong for where the site is headed. **Reframe**: a project is defined by two orthogonal axes: (a) **tech stack** â€” the software platform the work targets (kaplay, phaser, babylon.js, pixi.js, three.js, vanilla, next, etc.) and (b) **context framework** â€” the methodology + skills set wrapping the work (bare, GSD, GAD, and more as we add them). Plus a **domain** â€” what the project is actually building (game, API, CLI, docs site, etc.). GAD is a context framework treated like any other â€” it's not the home team, it's one of several. The site becomes an **interactive playground** for building and managing projects where the operator can swap tech stacks and context frameworks per project to see how they behave. Phase 44 (Project Market) and phase 44.5 (Brood Editor) absorb this framing: the marketplace lets users browse projects by (tech_stack, context_framework, domain); the editor lets users swap any of the three on a per-project basis. **Context frameworks are a new content type alongside skills, agents, workflows** â€” each framework has its own skill set, agent set, workflows, and canonical examples. GAD's own skills/agents/workflows stay where they are; they become the "GAD context framework" entries in a broader catalog.

### Impact

(1) New phase **42.4 â€” Context framework as first-class concept** inserted after 42.3. Scope: introduce a `ContextFramework` type in the site catalog (id, name, description, skills, agents, workflows, canonical-projects, version), register bare/GSD/GAD as the first three entries, extend /project-market to filter by context framework as well as tech stack, and prepare the Brood Editor to swap context framework on a per-project basis. Does NOT ship the brood editor itself (that's 44.5) â€” just the data model and catalog entries so 44.5 can consume them. (2) Phase 43's species schema unification becomes cleaner: a "species" is `(project, context_framework, tech_stack)` â€” three rows per project when the canonical matrix is bare+GSD+GAD. (3) Phase 45 rebrand should reflect the framework-agnostic framing in hero copy: the site is an evolutionary playground for trying frameworks and stacks against problems, not "the GAD marketing site." (4) The `evals/` directory structure already supports this â€” each eval project has its own config; we surface the context-framework axis explicitly. (5) No change required for decision gad-164's comparison matrix (bare/GSD/GAD/custom) â€” that is exactly the context-framework axis.

## gad-175: Trace events are scoped by context â€” GAD-framework vs eval-agent vs brood-project â€” and the workflow detector filters by scope

### Summary

The initial 42.3-04 run exposed a correctness problem: `.planning/.trace-events.jsonl` at the GAD repo root contains events from every cwd the hooks fire in, including events produced by agents working INSIDE eval worktrees (escape-the-dungeon, etc.). Those events are game-dev activity inside an eval's generation, not GAD-framework activity, and should not contribute to GAD's own emergent-workflow detection or `workflow_conformance` score. **Policy**: every trace event has a `scope` â€” `gad-framework` (real work on GAD itself), `eval-agent` (work inside an eval worktree or preservation path), `brood-project` (work inside a registered brood-editor project), or similar scopes added as new project types land. The workflow synthesis pipeline and `workflow_conformance` metric consume ONLY `gad-framework`-scoped events. Eval agents get their own trace files and their own synthesis runs scoped to their own generation. **v1 implementation** (shipped 42.3-04 revision): a path-based classifier (`classifyTraceEventScope`) runs at `loadTraceEvents()` ingest time â€” any event whose path contains `.claude/worktrees/`, `/worktrees/agent-*`, or `/evals/<project>/game/` is tagged `eval-agent`; everything else defaults to `gad-framework`. **v2**: explicit `scope` field written by the hooks based on cwd / worktree detection at emission time, so new scopes can be added without touching the classifier heuristic. The classifier stays as the fallback for legacy traces.

### Impact

(1) `site/scripts/build-site-data.mjs` now loads via `loadTraceEvents({ scope: "gad-framework" })` and reports both filtered and total counts so drift between scopes is visible. (2) New task **42.3-14** â€” hook-level scope tagging: `bin/gad-trace-hook.cjs` and `.claude/hooks/tool-trace.js` detect cwd / worktree at emission time and write `scope: "..."` into the JSONL record. (3) Brood-editor projects (phase 44.5) will use their own scope tag so the same synthesis pipeline can produce per-project workflow graphs without cross-contamination. (4) When the eval preservation contract runs (decision gad-38), the eval's own trace file is already preserved under `evals/<project>/v<N>/run/` â€” that stream is the canonical source for per-generation synthesis. Nothing about eval preservation changes.

## gad-174: Emergent workflow detection â€” DFG + frequent subgraph mining v1, proto-workflow as first-class permanent type, three-section visualization

### Summary

The trace synthesis pipeline (phase 42.3-04) must distinguish three things: (a) runs of authored workflows, (b) emergent workflows â€” recurring trace patterns nobody wrote down but that appear often enough to be real, (c) noise â€” one-off sequences that don't repeat. Four techniques considered: directly-follows graph + frequent subgraph mining (pure Node, deterministic), PrefixSpan / GSP sequence mining (handles gaps, deterministic), process mining via pm4py (Python sidecar, purpose-built for this field), LLM-over-traces (semantic, non-deterministic, expensive). Decision: **start with DFG + frequent subgraph mining as v1**. Pure Node, no new dependencies, deterministic and debuggable, and GAD's trace volumes (hundreds-to-thousands of events, not millions) don't justify anything heavier. Authored workflows already give ground truth to validate the detector against. If v1 output is noisy or misses obvious patterns, upgrade path is pm4py via a dev-only Python sidecar (analogous to the 44.5 brood-editor bridge). LLM detection is not a first-pass detector â€” at best a tertiary naming/describing pass layered on top of syntactic detection. **Signal vs noise gated by four configurable thresholds**: min_support (â‰¥3 distinct phases â€” one-offs are not workflows, two might be coincidence, three is a pattern); min_length (â‰¥3 nodes â€” 2-node sequences are just edges); edge_stability (â‰¥60% of matching instances â€” an edge that only shows up in one-third is intra-candidate noise); coverage (â‰¥20% of events per matching instance â€” a 3-event snippet in a 50-event trace is a fragment). Candidates that barely fail one threshold go in a "marginal" bucket (visible, flagged). **Proto-workflow is the workflow analog of proto-skill and is also a first-class permanent type** (mirrors decision gad-167). Pipeline shape identical: recurring trace pattern â†’ emergent-workflow candidate â†’ proto-workflow (`.planning/workflows/emergent/<slug>.md` with `origin: emergent` + `support: { phases, stability }` + `evidence: [trace_instance_ids]` frontmatter) â†’ promoted authored workflow (moved to `.planning/workflows/<slug>.md`, emergent metadata stripped, provenance retained). CLI surface mirrors gad evolution: `gad workflow status | validate | promote | discard`. A workflow born from the detector keeps its origin label forever so authored-vs-emergent is a first-class eval axis (same justification as proto-skill-vs-human-authored). **Visualization is three distinct sections on the Workflows tab**, not one: (1) Authored â€” solid edges, blue â€” the six hand-authored workflows with expected/actual/diff. (2) Emergent â€” dashed edges, green â€” proto-workflows above threshold with support-count badges, evidence trace IDs, and inline promote/discard actions. (3) Noise panel â€” separate surface with raw event timeline (filterable) and an unmatched force-directed graph for events the detector could not absorb into any candidate. Dense clusters in the noise view are "maybe a workflow, lower thresholds"; sparse clusters are genuine noise. The noise panel stays OFF the signal sections so signal surfaces remain readable.

### Impact

(1) Phase 42.3-04 scope is "DFG + frequent subgraph mining in pure Node reading from `.gad-log/` JSONL + `.trace-events.jsonl`, emitting candidate subgraphs with support counts and stability metrics." (2) New tasks queued: 42.3-09 emergent workflow detector v1; 42.3-10 proto-workflow filesystem layout + `.planning/workflows/emergent/` dir; 42.3-11 `gad workflow status|validate|promote|discard` CLI; 42.3-12 emergent section on /planning Workflows tab; 42.3-13 noise panel (deferred, not part of v1). (3) Detector thresholds are configurable via `.planning/config.json` so v1 can be tuned without code changes. (4) The upgrade path to pm4py is explicitly captured as a follow-up decision â€” v1 ships first and we evaluate against real trace data before adopting heavier tooling. (5) Proto-workflow pipeline reuses the evolution-loop conventions (PROVENANCE.md, advisory validator, permanent origin label, first-class eval axis) so users only learn one mental model, not two. (6) LLM-based workflow naming is a phase-46+ consideration â€” not blocking v1.

## gad-173: Workflow validator = expected/actual diff = next `framework_compliance` metric

### Summary

Three open questions about workflows (nesting, validators, self-report) resolve together because they all hinge on what a workflow validator actually IS. Answers: (a) **Workflows nest.** A workflow can declare `parent-workflow: <slug>` in frontmatter and will be rendered as a sub-graph inside its parent on /planning. `gad-decide` nests inside `gad-discuss-plan-execute`; `gad-debug` nests inside `gad-loop`. Mermaid supports subgraphs natively so the rendering is free. (b) **Workflows carry validators, and the validator IS the expected/actual graph diff.** There is no separate validator schema. For any completed phase or session, trace synthesis computes the actual graph; the validator diffs actual vs expected and emits a conformance score: `workflow_conformance = (matching_nodes - extra_nodes - out_of_order_nodes) / expected_nodes`. This score UPGRADES the current flat presence-based `framework_compliance` (gad-162) which only checks "did tasks get skill/agent/type attributes filled in." The new metric asks the harder question: "did the sequence and usage pattern match the designed workflow?" It stays advisory â€” no blocking, no auto-rollback â€” but it becomes the headline framework-discipline number on the site. (c) **Agents self-report workflow identity explicitly.** When a GAD-aware skill or agent begins work inside a recognized workflow, it emits a structured trace event (`workflow_enter: { slug, instance_id }` / `workflow_exit: { slug, instance_id, status }`) into `.trace-events.jsonl`. The synthesizer uses those events as authoritative hints when present and falls back to sequence inference from tool/skill/agent attribution when absent. Explicit is cheap (~3 extra trace events per workflow), makes the synthesis problem simple, and gives agents a place to say "I'm intentionally deviating because X." Fallback inference stays for legacy runs and non-GAD-aware skills.

### Impact

(1) Phase 42.3 scope explicitly includes upgrading `framework_compliance` to `workflow_conformance` once the first workflows are authored and the synthesis pipeline lands. The 2.2% number (gad-162 measurement) is a baseline on a metric we are replacing. (2) Workflow frontmatter gains `parent-workflow` (optional) and the /planning Workflows tab renders nested graphs via mermaid subgraphs. (3) Trace event schema gains `workflow_enter` / `workflow_exit` events; existing hooks (`.claude/hooks/tool-trace.js`, `bin/gad-trace-hook.cjs`) can emit them when a skill/agent declares it belongs to a workflow. Skills that participate in workflows declare the workflow slug in their frontmatter. (4) The validator is NOT a new file â€” it is a function in the same trace-analysis pipeline that already computes the actual graph. One function in, one score out. (5) Scoring weights (matching vs extra vs out-of-order) are deliberately simple for v1 and will be tuned as real runs accumulate. (6) When the diff shows EVERY run deviates the same way, the framework's expected workflow is probably wrong â€” that is a signal to rewrite the expected graph, not to punish the runs. When ONE run deviates, that run is worth investigating. This is the tuning loop the user wants.

## gad-172: Workflows are a first-class GAD concept â€” hand-authored expected graph, computed actual graph, diff as tuning signal

### Summary

GAD already traces skills, agents, and tool calls per phase (trace v4 from phases 25 and 32). That trace data is rich enough to compute an actual "workflow graph" â€” the sequence of skill invocations, agent spawns, and decision points an agent traversed to complete a task â€” without any new instrumentation. We should elevate "workflow" to a first-class concept alongside phase, plan, skill, and agent: hand-author the workflows we EXPECT (gad loop, discussâ†’planâ†’execute, evolution loop, debug loop, findings authoring, the pros/consâ†’recommendâ†’commit decision pattern) as mermaid diagrams with structured metadata, then compute the ACTUAL workflow from trace data for any completed phase or session, and render both side-by-side on the /planning page with a diff highlighting divergence (missing steps, extra steps, out-of-order steps). The goal is NOT enforcement â€” we are not blocking on deviation. The goal is to surface drift as a tuning signal for both the framework (when everyone deviates the same way, the expected workflow is wrong) and individual agents/humans (when one run deviates, that run is worth investigating). This unlocks a feedback loop we cannot get any other way: "is the framework actually being followed, and when it isn't, is that a framework problem or an execution problem?"

### Impact

(1) New planning artifact type: `workflows/*.md` (or similar) with a mermaid fenced block + frontmatter metadata (name, description, expected participants: which skills/agents appear, trigger conditions). Schema TBD in phase 42.3. (2) `build-site-data.mjs` gains a workflow extractor emitting a new entry in catalog.generated.ts. (3) Trace analysis (existing `trace-analysis` skill / .gad-log infra) gains a "workflow synthesis" step that groups trace events into workflow nodes. (4) `/planning` gets a Workflows tab rendering expected graph (from hand-authored) + actual graph (from trace) + diff. Mermaid renders client-side. (5) First workflows to author: gad loop, gad evolution, gad decide (the pros/consâ†’recommendâ†’commit pattern the user validated in this conversation), gad debug, gad discussâ†’planâ†’execute, gad findings authoring. (6) No enforcement in this phase â€” advisory signal only, same discipline as the evolution validator. (7) This is a natural next step after 42.2 (proto-skill formalization) because both rely on the same question: "is what the agent did aligned with what the framework expected?"

## gad-171: `create-proto-skill` uses bulk batching with per-candidate checkpoints

### Summary

When implemented, `create-proto-skill` will process all pending candidates in a single invocation rather than one-per-run. Rationale: the skill-creation context (dot-agent format rules, example skills, reference docs) is large and reloading it for every candidate wastes tokens at scale â€” at N=14 candidates with ~10k of skill-creation references, per-invocation mode costs ~140k of repeated hydration overhead. Bulk mode amortizes that cost across N drafts and also enables cross-candidate pattern recognition (noticing that two candidates want the same underlying skill and merging them) which is impossible one-at-a-time. The tradeoffs â€” higher blast radius on mid-run drift and resumability â€” are mitigated by a per-candidate checkpoint discipline: write `PROVENANCE.md` FIRST as a lock marker, then `SKILL.md`, then move on; on any startup the skill enumerates pending candidates and skips any whose proto-skill dir already has both files. A partial crash leaves at most one half-written proto-skill; everything before that is complete and everything after is unstarted. This gets us amortized context and resumability at the cost of ~20 lines of orchestration inside the skill.

### Impact

(1) `skills/create-proto-skill/SKILL.md` documents the bulk+checkpoint workflow explicitly. (2) The skill reads `skills/candidates/` directory at startup, filters to candidates without a matching `.planning/proto-skills/<slug>/PROVENANCE.md + SKILL.md` pair, drafts each in turn. (3) Review-burst pain (N proto-skills landing at once) is handled by the existing /planning proto-skills tab, which is already designed to triage a queue. (4) `gad evolution status` must render pending/in-progress/complete counts so partial runs are visible at a glance. (5) This decision overrides the earlier ambiguity left in task 42.2-06.

## gad-170: Brood Editor is local-dev-only â€” no Vercel npx execution in the first cut

### Summary

The brood editor / project editor surface (phase 44.5) will assume local development for its first implementation. It may read the filesystem, trigger `npx` commands, populate eval folders, and rebuild pages via a dev-server-only bridge. We are explicitly NOT shipping a Vercel-hosted API route that runs `npx` or mutates the filesystem in production. Vercel Sandbox could support this later but adds operational weight (cold starts, security boundaries, cost) that is not justified until the local editor proves the concept. A small, pragmatic dev-server bridge keeps the iteration loop fast and the surface area small.

### Impact

(1) Phase 44.5 plan assumes a Node child-process bridge reachable only when `NODE_ENV=development`, rejecting all mutation requests in production builds. (2) No new dependencies on Vercel-specific runtimes. (3) Production /project-market remains read-only browsing; the editor is a local-dev superset. (4) If we later decide the editor should work in prod, that becomes a separate phase explicitly evaluating Vercel Sandbox vs a dedicated backend.

## gad-169: Findings are articles / whitepapers â€” first-class nav item with per-slug pages

### Summary

/findings is reframed from an internal scratchpad into a public articles/whitepapers surface. Each finding gets its own standalone page at /findings/[slug] so they can be linked from other site pages (LineageMeasurementSection, RunHeroCallouts, rubric hero, etc.) and shared externally without deep-anchor workarounds. The /findings index remains the discovery page, and the Reference nav group keeps Findings as a first-class entry alongside Glossary and Decisions. Framing findings as whitepapers clarifies the editorial bar: each finding documents an experiment, what we tried, what we learned, and what changed in the architecture.

### Impact

(1) New route: `site/app/findings/[slug]/page.tsx` using `generateStaticParams` over the FINDINGS catalog. Fixes the 404 that existing /findings/&lt;slug&gt; links produced. (2) Nav already lists Findings under Reference (gad-157); no nav change needed. (3) Editorial convention: treat new findings as articles, give them summaries, surface them on the /findings index with badges for round/version/projects. (4) Phase 45 rebrand should include a pass over the Findings index copy to reinforce the whitepaper framing.

## gad-168: Rename `gad-quick-skill` â†’ `create-proto-skill`; neutral upstream baseline stays untouched

### Summary

`gad-quick-skill` was created as a GAD-aware wrapper around an upstream neutral skill creator, but the wrapping pulled GAD context into what was meant to be a baseline-neutral surface. The neutral baseline (dot-agent `create-skill` or similar) should remain untouched for non-GAD projects; the GAD wrapper is its own skill with its own name. Going forward the GAD wrapper is called `create-proto-skill`, which accurately describes what it does: it consumes a candidate handoff and produces a staged proto-skill under `.planning/proto-skills/`. The skill uses a filesystem-based input contract (CANDIDATE.md, optional CONTEXT.md, optional REFERENCES.md) so it can batch many candidates in one invocation and amortize its own context load. It does not assume pre-hydrated GAD context â€” if it needs phase/decision/reference data it calls `gad snapshot` / `gad decisions` / `gad phases` itself.

### Impact

(1) Rename `skills/gad-quick-skill/` â†’ `skills/create-proto-skill/` and update SKILL.md title/description. (2) Update `skills/gad-evolution-evolve/` to call `create-proto-skill` by the new name. (3) Any references in AGENTS.md, site copy, or docs get updated in the same commit. (4) `gad-skill-creator` remains the high-stakes human-in-the-loop skill creator; the two are different tools with different stakes, documented in `references/proto-skills.md`. (5) Not urgent â€” can ship as part of phase 42.2 follow-up work.

## gad-167: Proto-skill is a permanent skill type, not a transitional state

### Summary

Any skill that was drafted by the evolution pipeline (from a candidate, via `create-proto-skill`) is permanently labeled a proto-skill, even after validation, promotion to `skills/`, or wide distribution. The label is provenance, not lifecycle stage. Rationale: when a skill starts misbehaving the fastest triage path is "which candidate + pressure event did this come from, and has that pressure reappeared?" That link is only useful if it survives promotion. It also lets us run proto-skill-vs-human-authored-skill as a first-class eval axis to measure whether the evolution loop actually produces competitive skills. Promoted proto-skills carry a `PROVENANCE.md` alongside `SKILL.md` recording candidate slug, phase id, pressure metrics, and creation timestamp.

### Impact

Formalized in `references/proto-skills.md`. The evolution pipeline stages (candidate â†’ proto-skill â†’ validated â†’ promoted) are all proto-skill stages; promotion does not strip the label. Skill metadata and catalog entries gain a provenance field so the site can filter human-authored vs proto-skill vs promoted-proto-skill. Replaces the earlier framing that proto-skill was just a step between candidate and `skills/`.

## gad-166: GAD is content-driven + emergent in one track â€” there is no separate "GAD+emergent" condition

### Summary

GAD is positioned from here on as a content-driven framework whose skills evolve via the evolution loop. The earlier "bare-vs-GAD-vs-emergent" framing implied three separate philosophies; in practice GAD has always included baseline content (skills, agents, references) and now also includes emergent skill creation on top of that content. The canonical matrix (gad-164) treats GAD as its own condition, GSD as its own condition, bare as the no-framework control, and `custom` as the umbrella for any content injection beyond a canonical baseline. Marketing copy, site hero, and /findings writeups should reflect this: GAD = content + evolutionary skill generation, not "GAD OR emergent."

### Impact

Rebrand copy (phase 45) and the framework comparison writeups must stop using "GAD+emergent" as a standalone condition. GAD's baseline DNA already includes the evolution loop; anything additional is `custom`. The evolution-themed vocabulary applies to GAD itself, not to a separate variant of it.

## gad-165: Upstream GSD stays a living comparison target, not just historical inspiration

### Summary

GAD is a derivative of GSD and should keep reviewing, testing, and selectively integrating upstream ideas rather than pretending the lineage is complete. The upstream mirror in `tmp/get-shit-done/` and periodic review notes are therefore not one-off research artifacts; they are part of the framework-comparison apparatus. Before porting worktree, workstream, SDK, security, or mode ideas from upstream, GAD should compare the live implementation, document parity/pros-cons, and then adapt the idea to GAD's own model instead of inheriting it blindly.

### Impact

Upstream review remains an explicit planning responsibility. Worktree/workstream parity decisions should cite concrete upstream commits and local review notes rather than memory or broad summaries.

## gad-164: Official framework comparison matrix: bare vs GSD vs GAD vs emergent vs custom

### Summary

The eval matrix should compare framework conditions cleanly. Canonical conditions are: **bare** (minimal project, no framework content), **GSD** (upstream get-shit-done framework), **GAD** (current get-anything-done framework), **emergent** (starts bare, gains agent-authored skills over time), and **custom** (any condition with extra installed skills/content beyond its canonical baseline). `GAD+emergent` is not a standalone canonical matrix condition anymore; if GAD receives extra skill/content injections beyond its baseline DNA, that generation is categorized as custom. Escape the Dungeon remains the official comparison project because it already anchors the lineage and gating history.

### Impact

Eval docs, future site comparisons, and project/species metadata should treat custom as the umbrella for injected-content variants. Official comparisons should be repeatable across the same project target and baseline definitions.

## gad-163: Hydration is the framework-overhead metric: snapshot count, snapshot tokens, and hydration share of total tokens

### Summary

The framework-overhead question is not "did the session start with snapshot," it is "how much context-hydration work did the framework require to keep the loop moving?" GAD should track hydration directly: how many snapshots happened, the estimated token volume of those snapshots, and the ratio `hydration_tokens / total_project_tokens`. This becomes the first-class context-overhead metric for framework comparison. It can coexist with planning-vs-source file-op overhead, but it answers the more important question about token spend.

### Impact

Self-eval data, CLI output, and planning-system UI should expose snapshot count, snapshot token totals, combined project tokens, and hydration percentage. Documentation should describe this as context overhead, not ritual compliance.

## gad-162: Retire `loop_compliance`; measure framework compliance through task attribution instead

### Summary

The existing self-eval `loop_compliance` metric is just `sessions starting with snapshot / total sessions`. That measures startup ritual, not whether the framework was actually used correctly throughout the work. For GAD itself, the more honest discipline signal is whether completed tasks in TASK-REGISTRY carry the required framework attribution (`skill`, `agent`, `type`) and whether planning artifacts were updated coherently. That metric should be named `framework_compliance`, not `loop_compliance`, to reflect what it is actually testing.

### Impact

Self-eval CLI, site data, and planning copy should stop treating snapshot-first behavior as the primary compliance score. Task attribution coverage becomes the first-class framework-discipline signal.

## gad-161: `workspace`, `worktree`, and `workstream` are different concepts and must stay distinct

### Summary

Current confusion comes from three unrelated ideas sharing similar names. In GAD: `workspace` means registered planning roots in the CLI config; `worktree` means a git working copy (often isolated for evals, but potentially useful for normal project work too); `workstream` means a scoped execution lane over shared planning artifacts. `gad workspace` did not replace git worktrees, and GAD workstreams are intentionally not upstream `.planning/workstreams/&lt;name&gt;/` copies. Future UX/docs must keep those meanings separate and explain the tradeoffs explicitly.

### Impact

CLI descriptions, docs, and future commands should use the three names consistently. Project worktree support can expand independently of the shared-tree workstream model.

## gad-160: `gad-config.toml` is the only canonical config; legacy config formats are compatibility layers

### Summary

GAD should not ask operators to reason about multiple source-of-truth config files. `gad-config.toml` is the canonical configuration file going forward. `planning-config.toml` remains read-only fallback for older repos and migration periods, but new writes target `gad-config.toml` only. `.planning/config.json` remains solely as a compatibility artifact for older workflow/tools surfaces that still expect JSON; it should be generated automatically from canonical config instead of hand-maintained. This keeps the operator mental model simple while preserving backward compatibility where the framework still needs it.

### Impact

Config-mutating codepaths should write TOML first and export compatibility JSON as needed. Docs and CLI help must stop implying equal authority between gad-config.toml, planning-config.toml, and config.json.

## gad-159: Magicborn per-project nav scoping is a pending design decision (phase 42 item 9)

### Summary

Magicborn needs the GAD site compile path to produce a pared-down nav: /planning as the home page, System tab kept, Catalog items merged into System, and the Evaluation / Theory / Play / Emergent / Skeptic tabs all dropped. During the phase 42 overnight push this was deferred because implementing it requires design decisions that shouldn't be made autonomously: (a) where the per-project site config lives â€” per-project `gad-config.toml [site]` table, standalone `magicborn/.planning/site.toml`, or top-level monorepo `[docs.projects.&lt;id&gt;.site]` table; (b) whether projects start with the full GAD nav and drop entries, or start blank and add them; (c) what happens at runtime when `GAD_PROJECT_ID` is unset (the main GAD site build). Implementation shape when resolved: build-site-data.mjs reads the per-project config and emits site-config.generated.ts keyed by GAD_PROJECT_ID; Nav components read it to conditionally render tabs/groups; extractPlanningSite filters the static compile file list. Until resolved, magicborn renders with the full GAD nav.

### Impact

Open design item tracked for the next session. Captured in oneoff/OVERNIGHT-NOTES.md with the three schema-location options. Not blocking phase 43+44+45 â€” the domain rename and marketplace work doesn't depend on per-project nav scoping.

## gad-158: Phase 45 rebrand is confirmed direction â€” full visual + copy overhaul around evolutionary vocabulary

### Summary

After the domain rename (phase 43) and marketplace redesign (phase 44) land, the site gets a dedicated visual + copy pass (phase 45) that re-anchors the brand around the evolutionary vocabulary (decision gad-151). This is not a parked "maybe someday" â€” the user explicitly confirmed "branding is changing too" on 2026-04-13 and wants the rebrand tracked as a first-class deliverable. Phase 45 touches: type ramp, color palette, hero copy, About page, README, external-facing docs, logo + favicon if needed, and a site-wide consistency audit so the same word is used for the same concept everywhere. The rebrand should land playfully â€” e.g. "the bare species' v4 generation hatched a spawn that crushed the v4 brood" â€” not academically. Logo/favicon refresh is in scope if the visual identity needs to change to match the new naming.

### Impact

Phase 45 depends on phases 43 + 44 and is a focused design + copy effort, not a code refactor. Findings and planning docs authored before phase 45 may need a consistency pass when the rebrand ships. FINDINGS-2026-04-13-evolution-loop-experiment already uses the correct vocabulary and is rebrand-ready.

## gad-157: System nav group uses four submenus â€” Internals, Catalog, Reference, Engage

### Summary

The System dropdown got too heavy (11 items in one flat list). It is now refactored into four subcategories rendered via Radix DropdownMenuSub: **Internals** (Planning, Requirements, Local DB), **Catalog** (Skills, Agents, Commands, Downloads), **Reference** (Lineage, Standards, Findings), **Engage** (Security, Contribute, Feature Requests). nav-shared.ts NavGroup is a discriminated union: either flat `links` or nested `subGroups` (NavSubGroup[]). NavDesktop renders both shapes via DropdownMenuSub/SubTrigger/SubContent; NavMobileMenu renders nested groups as inline labeled sections (no flyout on mobile). The Findings entry was added to Reference as part of restoring the /findings route that was dropped in an earlier cleanup â€” framework-level findings need somewhere to live, and project-specific findings continue to appear on project detail pages via ProjectFindingsSection.

### Impact

Nav grows without overwhelming a single dropdown. Future nav additions default to picking a subcategory rather than flattening. nav-shared.ts type changes ripple to NavDesktop.tsx, NavMobileMenu.tsx, and any other consumer.

## gad-156: New evolutions gate on zero pending proto-skills (the human review gate)

### Summary

gad-evolution-evolve refuses to start a new evolution if .planning/proto-skills/ contains any pending proto-skills from a previous evolution. Rationale: evolutions accumulating without review just rot â€” if candidates stack up faster than humans review them, the whole loop becomes noise. The gate forces the user to clear the queue (`gad evolution promote &lt;slug&gt;` or `gad evolution discard &lt;slug&gt;`) before a new evolution can begin. There is no `--force` flag because forcing this defeats the gate. An evolution implicitly closes when `.planning/proto-skills/` is empty again.

### Impact

Evolutions stay bounded. The proto-skills directory IS the work list â€” no separate task-per-candidate bloat. A single TASK-REGISTRY entry tracks the review obligation for the whole evolution batch. skills/.evolutions/&lt;YYYY-MM-DD-NNN&gt; marker files persist the evolution timeline even after all proto-skills are reviewed and gone.

## gad-155: Validator is advisory, not blocking â€” failures flag, human decides

### Summary

The gad-evolution-validator skill and its engine (lib/evolution-validator.cjs) check proto-skill SKILL.md files for cited file references that don't exist in the repo and cited `gad &lt;subcommand&gt;` commands that don't appear in `gad --help`. Output lands in VALIDATION.md alongside the SKILL.md with a pass/fail table per check. Failures do not block promotion â€” they surface gaps for the human reviewer before they run `gad evolution promote`. Rationale: a forward-looking skill may cite paths or commands that don't exist yet because the skill documents something still being built. Blocking on those would false-positive too often. The human is the gate. v1 validator covers file-exists and CLI-command-recognized checks; JSON shape comparison (e.g. does the skill's example gad.json match real ones?) is deferred until needed.

### Impact

`gad evolution validate &lt;slug&gt;` writes VALIDATION.md. gad-evolution-evolve calls the validator after each draft. The human reviewer reads VALIDATION.md alongside SKILL.md during review. Known v2 improvement logged: dedup file refs that appear under multiple path prefixes (~10 line fix in lib/evolution-validator.cjs extractFileRefs).

## gad-154: Two-tier skill creators â€” gad-quick-skill (default) and gad-skill-creator (high-stakes)

### Summary

GAD maintains two drafting wrappers with different trade-offs. **gad-quick-skill** wraps dot-agent's create-skill (https://github.com/siviter-xyz/dot-agent) unmodified â€” a lightweight authoring guide with the 200-line rule, progressive disclosure principle, and references/ split. No eval loop, no subagent test runs, no benchmarks. Universal use (not under the evolution: namespace) so it's available for any fast skill drafting. **gad-skill-creator** wraps Anthropic's full skill-creator (https://github.com/anthropics/skills) â€” the heavy path with subagent test runs, grading, benchmarks, viewer.html, and iteration. Reserved for skills that need real test validation before promotion. The evolution loop defaults to quick-skill because most high-pressure-phase candidates don't need the test harness to produce a useful draft. Users override to the heavy path when the skill is load-bearing enough that the extra cost is worth it.

### Impact

Two GAD skills in `skills/`: gad-quick-skill (default, invoked by gad-evolution-evolve) and gad-skill-creator (opt-in). Both take candidate context as input and write SKILL.md output; only the heavy one runs additional validation internally.

## gad-153: Raw candidate input beats curated INTENT â€” the curator is a filter, not an amplifier

### Summary

Two controlled experiments on 2026-04-13 compared feeding raw phase data vs a hand-curated structured INTENT.md to skill drafting tools. Result flipped between heavy (Anthropic skill-creator) and light (dot-agent create-skill) drafters: with the light tool, the RAW arm pulled 16 decisions vs the curator arm's 7. The curator turned out to filter out load-bearing details (trace schema fragment, runtime identity, per-eval-repo architecture). A parallel test-loop experiment also showed baseline agents reading the actual repo produced more accurate gad.json shapes than agents following the skill's prescribed shape. Combined conclusion: curators are filters, not amplifiers. Skills should describe what IS, not what SHOULD BE. Drafting tools should read raw context and decide what matters, not consume pre-digested human opinions.

### Impact

Dropped the INTENT.md curator step from the evolution loop. compute-self-eval writes raw CANDIDATE.md. gad-evolution-evolve does not invoke any curator skill. Full experimental writeup at evals/FINDINGS-2026-04-13-evolution-loop-experiment.md with tables for both arms and the methodology caveats.

## gad-152: Two-stage evolution pipeline â€” candidate â†’ proto-skill â†’ skill

### Summary

Skill creation flows through three distinct stages, each in its own directory. **Candidate** (skills/candidates/&lt;slug&gt;/CANDIDATE.md) is the raw phase dump produced by compute-self-eval when a phase exceeds the selection pressure threshold â€” no curator pre-digestion, just task list + decisions + file refs + CLI surface for the drafter to discover from. **Proto-skill** (.planning/proto-skills/&lt;slug&gt;/SKILL.md + references/ + VALIDATION.md) is what the drafter produces; awaits human review. **Skill** (skills/&lt;final&gt;/) is promoted and joins the species DNA. Promotion and discard are file moves, not skills â€” `gad evolution promote &lt;slug&gt;` and `gad evolution discard &lt;slug&gt;` are CLI subcommands alongside `status` and `validate`. The stage is tracked by directory presence, not frontmatter: if proto-skills/&lt;slug&gt;/ exists, the candidate has been drafted and compute-self-eval does not churn it.

### Impact

compute-self-eval.mjs stops writing SKILL.md stubs and writes CANDIDATE.md instead. gad-evolution-evolve orchestrates the pipeline. gad-quick-skill reads candidates and writes proto-skills. gad-evolution-validator writes VALIDATION.md advisories next to proto-skill SKILL.md. skills/candidates/ and .planning/proto-skills/ are both indexed on the site /planning page as sibling tabs.

## gad-151: Evolutionary domain vocabulary â€” species, generation, brood, evolution, DNA, spawn

### Summary

The GAD eval domain adopts a single locked evolutionary vocabulary. A **species** is a workflow lineage inside a project (bare, gad, emergent). A **generation** is one versioned run within a species (v1, v2â€¦). A **brood** is all generations across species at the same round. An **evolution** is the timeline of broods. **DNA** is the skill manifest baked into a species â€” the set of skills installed when an agent runs under that species. **Spawn** is everything a generation produces: the build, planning docs, trace, commits, and review bundle. Projects remain the top-level marketable unit; species live **inside** projects (phase 43 schema unification). Framework self-development uses the same vocabulary â€” building a new skill is evolving GAD's own species DNA.

### Impact

All user-facing copy (site nav, hero, docs, CLI help, findings, README) must use these exact terms. Phase 43 executes the rename across the codebase and site display strings. Phase 45 re-anchors visual branding around the same vocabulary. Old terms (rounds, eval projects, eval runs) survive only in internal file paths where renaming would break eval preservation artifacts.

## gad-150: Agent lane depth is capped at one direct child level

### Summary

GAD adopts a hard concurrency depth limit of `1`. Root agents may spawn direct child lanes, but child lanes may not spawn additional children. This preserves the single-master-loop model, prevents recursive agent explosion, keeps trace lineage understandable, and avoids hidden token burn from swarm-like recursion. Parent/root lineage is still recorded explicitly, but the lineage graph is always root plus zero-or-more direct children.

### Impact

`gad snapshot` and future assignment/spawn helpers must reject `parent-agentid` chains that would create depth `2` or deeper. Trace data and site reporting only need to support root/child lineage, not arbitrary trees.

## gad-149: gad snapshot is the normal agent-lane bootstrap handshake

### Summary

Concurrent GAD agents should not need a separate mandatory registration command before they can work. `gad snapshot` is both the context loader and the identity/bootstrap handshake. When snapshot is called with runtime/role/parent/task/phase context, or when a known runtime is detected, it auto-registers or refreshes the calling lane, returns `agent`, `scope`, and `assignments` blocks, and persists live lane state in the project planning directory. Manual registration can remain a debugging/ops affordance later, but it is not the canonical workflow.

### Impact

All runtime integrations can start from one call: snapshot. Spawned subagents receive their lane identity and scoped context from the same command. Shared lane state becomes machine-managed in `.planning/.gad-agent-lanes.json`.

## gad-148: Query-oriented SDK is the canonical planning logic core; CLI remains the canonical operator surface

### Summary

GAD keeps a CLI-first operator model for skills and humans, but the framework should stop treating subprocess calls to `gad-tools` as the hidden source of truth for planning operations. The canonical logic layer should be a query-oriented SDK surface (`sdk/src/query/...`) that exposes typed in-process handlers for state, roadmap, tasks, scoped snapshots, and task assignment. The CLI then becomes a thin wrapper over those handlers. Skills still invoke the CLI because that keeps agent behavior explicit and reproducible. Pipelines, site generation, trace/reporting, and future runtime adapters should use the query layer directly when possible.

### Impact

Reduces duplication, improves testability, and makes future concurrency features like scoped snapshots and task claims easier to ship without inventing a second planning filesystem. Separates methodology skills (CLI-facing) from deterministic automation/pipelines (query-facing).

## gad-147: GAD workstreams are scoped execution contexts over shared planning artifacts, not duplicated planning trees

### Summary

After reviewing upstream get-shit-done workstreams, GAD adopts a different direction. The concurrency problem is real, but duplicating `STATE`, `ROADMAP`, `REQUIREMENTS`, and `phases/` under `.planning/workstreams/&lt;name&gt;/` is not the right fit for GAD's eval, tracing, and site-reporting model. GAD workstreams should instead be execution lanes over a single canonical planning tree. Shared planning artifacts remain the source of truth. Context is narrowed by scoped snapshots (`--phaseid`, `--taskid`, later optional `--agentid`) and by explicit task/phase assignment metadata.

### Impact

Future workstream support in GAD will be built around scoped snapshots and assignment state, not namespaced planning files. Docs and workflows must not imply upstream-style `/gad:workstreams` support unless and until this model is implemented.

## gad-146: Claude-facing model profiles stay off by default and use runtime-safe aliases when enabled

### Summary

Model profiles were present in prompts and references but had drifted out of the live workflow: gad-tools init no longer emitted the resolved model fields, the SDK default still assumed an always-on profile in some places, and the SDK path used a stale frozen Haiku model ID that failed at runtime. Policy: (1) `model_profile` defaults to `off` so GAD does not silently change models unless the operator opts in. (2) When enabled for Claude-facing runtimes, GAD passes Claude aliases (`opus`, `sonnet`, `haiku`, or `inherit`) instead of frozen full model IDs, allowing the runtime to resolve to a current accessible version. (3) Model switching must be considered real only when verified by runtime events such as Claude SessionInit, not just by prompt interpolation or unit tests. (4) Shared resolver logic lives in GAD and feeds both workflow init payloads and SDK/session paths, so prompt expectations and actual spawn behavior stay aligned.

### Impact

gad-tools init now emits resolved workflow model fields again. Claude SDK sessions no longer fail on a stale Haiku model ID and can be live-verified through SessionInit.model. Docs and tests move from balanced-by-default assumptions to off-by-default semantics. Future runtime-identity work in phase 35 builds on a working model-selection baseline instead of dead fields.

## gad-145: Candidate review lifecycle + configurable pressure formula + Shannon framing

### Summary

Three fixes layered on phase 38 candidate generation. (1) Candidate review lifecycle: each candidate SKILL.md gets a reviewed frontmatter field (default false). A human sets reviewed to promoted, merged, or discarded with reviewed_on and reviewed_notes. The pipeline skips any candidate whose reviewed is not false â€” reviewed candidates are not regenerated. Eliminates the "12 candidates regenerate forever" problem. (2) Configurable pressure formula: hardcoded crosscut_weight = 2 and high_pressure_threshold = 10 become entries in site/data/self-eval-config.json. Pressure formula is pressure = tasks_total + (crosscuts * crosscut_weight). Defaults preserved. Formula becomes tunable as we learn what pressure levels correlate with useful skill candidates. (3) Shannon-inspired framing documented on /formulas site page: tasks_total represents raw complexity of a phase (H entropy approximation), crosscuts represent mutual-information between subsystems (tasks spanning multiple systems indicate cross-cutting concerns), and the weighted sum approximates total information burden the phase imposed on agents. Crosscuts weighted heavily because cross-cutting work is disproportionately hard â€” a task spanning two systems requires context from both, roughly doubling the mental state agents must hold simultaneously.

### Impact

Candidates have review state + quarantine persistence. site/data/self-eval-config.json drives weights. /formulas site page documents pressure math and Shannon framing. compute-self-eval.mjs reads config at run time. PlanningSkillCandidatesTab gains Unreviewed/Reviewed filter. Phase 38 retroactively updated to ship these three pieces.

## gad-144: Skill candidates â€” auto-author draft skills from high-pressure phases, quarantine for review

### Summary

When the self-eval pipeline flags a phase as high-pressure (task count + crosscuts above threshold), that's a signal patterns exist that could become skills (per GAD-D-115). Rather than just logging the flag, the pipeline should auto-generate draft SKILL.md files inspired by the phase's tasks and park them in .agents/skills/candidates/ under the System category. Candidates are NOT available to GAD â€” they have status: candidate in frontmatter and are excluded from the catalog scan. A new /planning tab "Skill candidates" shows them for human review, with each candidate showing: the skill draft content, the source phase it came from, the list of tasks in that phase, and a pressure score. An info icon with HoverCard explains what a candidate is and how it's generated. Reviewer can then: promote the candidate to a real skill (moves to .agents/skills/&lt;name&gt;/), merge it into an existing skill via merge-skill, or discard it. This turns the high-pressure observation into actionable skill creation without polluting the live catalog with untested drafts.

### Impact

New .agents/skills/candidates/ directory (gitignored from catalog scan). New planning tab "Skill candidates" on /planning. Candidate detail page shows source phase + tasks. Self-eval pipeline extended to emit candidate drafts for phases above pressure threshold. Info icon HoverCard on the tab explains the feedback loop from pressure to skill creation. Phase 38 implements this.

## gad-143: gad env status / gad env agents â€” runtime availability checks

### Summary

Before any eval operation (start/auto/finish), the CLI must verify that required runtimes are available. gad env status prints a table of: Claude Code CLI (claude), Codex CLI (codex), Cursor Agent (cursor-agent), GitHub CLI (gh) + auth status, Node version, git version. Missing tools show install links. gad env agents is a shorter alias focused on just coding agent runtimes. gad eval auto fails fast with a helpful error if the requested runtime is not installed or gh is not authenticated. Install links: claude via docs.anthropic.com, codex via github.com/openai/codex, cursor-agent via cursor.com/install, gh via cli.github.com.

### Impact

New top-level gad env command with status + agents subcommands. Eval commands gain a preflight check that calls into the same detection logic. Prevents cryptic command-not-found errors mid-eval. Documented in quick-start.md as step 0.

## gad-142: Skill/agent tracking is GAD-workflow-only, not universal

### Summary

The skill and agent attributes on TASK-REGISTRY.xml tasks (decision GAD-D-104) only apply when the agent is using the full GAD workflow. Bare and emergent conditions have minimal or agent-defined planning artifacts â€” forcing skill tracking on them would contaminate the Freedom/CSH hypothesis measurements. Non-GAD evals are evaluated on outcomes only: git diffs, file mutations, commit rhythm, build artifacts, token usage, duration, human review. The per-tool-use hook (gad-trace-hook.cjs) still captures tool_use/file_mutation/subagent_spawn events for Claude Code regardless of condition, but skill_invocation events from that hook are dead code (the .trace-active-skill marker file was designed but never wired into any skill, so trigger_skill is always null in the trace). Phase 36 removes the dead code.

### Impact

Eval guide and project-market docs clarify the observability tradeoff: GAD gives you skill-level visibility in exchange for overhead; bare gives you outcome-only scoring. gad-trace-hook.cjs pruned of skill_invocation logic in phase 36. The trace-active-skill concept retired as never-implemented. Trace events documented as tool-level always, skill-level GAD-only via planning-artifact attribution.

## gad-141: gad eval suite replaced by gad project run-round

### Summary

The batch eval runner is now project-centric. Instead of gad eval suite (which operates on arbitrary eval project lists), use gad project run-round --projectid &lt;id&gt; --roundnumber &lt;N&gt; [--runtime codex|claude|cursor] which executes all eval variants in the specified round for the specified project. Multiple project ids can be passed for parallel execution. Without --runtime, generates prompts only (start mode for each). With --runtime, runs each via auto mode and streams. This matches the project-round-eval hierarchy and stops requiring users to track eval ids manually.

### Impact

gad eval suite command removed (or kept as a thin alias during migration). New gad project run-round implementation. Works only after gad project command lands (phase 37 or later). Until then, gad eval auto --project X is the imperative form.

## gad-140: gad eval run â†’ start / auto / finish â€” explicit command split

### Summary

The existing gad eval run command conflates scaffolding with execution. Split into three commands: gad eval start (scaffold run dir, create eval repo clone if needed, generate PROMPT.md + TRACE.json, hand off to external agent â€” matches current "run --execute" behavior), gad eval auto (scaffold THEN shell out to a coding agent CLI via --runtime codex|claude|cursor, stream output, wait, automatically call finish â€” closes the loop), gad eval finish (preserve outputs, push to eval repo, compute sizes/scores, mark run done â€” matches current preserve). gad eval run itself is deprecated but kept as an alias for gad eval start to preserve existing scripts. Naming rationale: "run" is ambiguous (begin a run vs execute a run); "auto" is unambiguous (autonomous end-to-end).

### Impact

CLI surface grows but becomes less ambiguous. eval-run skill is renamed/rewritten to eval-start. eval-bootstrap skill becomes eval-auto. eval-preserve becomes an internal step of finish. Docs updated. Suite runs become gad project run-round which expands to parallel auto calls.

## gad-139: Per-eval-repo architecture â€” one GitHub repo per eval project, directories for runs

### Summary

Each eval project (e.g. escape-the-dungeon-bare) gets its own GitHub repository (private initially). Runs live as directories inside the repo: round-N/vM/. The main monorepo only holds eval metadata (gad.json + lightweight TRACE.json reference), not the actual source/build artifacts. This prevents the monorepo from ballooning as brownfield codebases and content packs are injected. gad eval start/auto clones/creates the eval repo, strips to just the current run's scaffold, generates PROMPT.md + TRACE.json. gad eval finish pushes the run back to the eval repo and updates the main repo's metadata reference. gad env status checks github auth + runtime availability before any eval operation. Supersedes the inline evals/project/vN/run/ layout for new evals; existing 49 inline runs stay as legacy until migrated.

### Impact

Eval projects become real repos with their own history. Main repo stays lean. Scoped clones for new runs â€” agents see only their own worktree, not the full eval history. Requires gh auth and runtime checks. Site data pipeline splits: metadata from main repo, artifacts fetched from eval repos as needed. Phase 36 implements this; phase 37 or later handles legacy run migration.

## gad-138: Standalone gad executables use Node SEA with an injected support payload

### Summary

The executable distribution for gad is implemented as a standalone binary built with Node SEA and postject. The binary embeds a packaged support payload containing the CLI bundle, installer entrypoint, hooks, commands, templates, workflows, references, agents, and skills. On first run, the executable extracts that payload into a versioned runtime directory under the user's profile and dispatches the extracted CLI or installer from there. End users do not need Node installed to run the packaged gad executable.

### Impact

Release scripts, install flows, and docs assume a packaged-runtime model rather than a loose JS bundle. Packaged builds can self-install globally on Windows and keep their shipped support assets versioned together.

## gad-137: Runtime identity in eval telemetry is runtime-derived first, self-declared second

### Summary

Eval telemetry must distinguish the coding agent runtime that produced a run: Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and future supported agents. The preferred source is runtime-derived metadata from hooks, config, or environment. A secondary self-declared identity may be recorded by the agent at task/session start, but self-report alone is not sufficient for trustworthy evaluation data. TRACE.json and related logs should preserve runtime name, model when available, token/tool/skill/agent usage, and provenance for how the identity was determined. Claude Code and Codex are the first-class targets; the schema should remain extensible for additional runtimes.

### Impact

TRACE.json, raw logs, and reporting need explicit runtime fields and provenance. Hook wiring and install flows must preserve enough metadata to attribute runs to a specific coding agent runtime so Claude vs Codex comparisons are trustworthy.

## gad-136: Executable release overhead is gated to shipped CLI surface changes only

### Summary

Building and publishing new GAD executables should happen only when the shipped CLI surface changes. Triggering inputs include changes under bin/, lib/, hooks/, package.json, build/release scripts, or any file bundled into the released artifact. Site content, eval results, planning docs, starters, and other non-shipped repo changes must not force a new executable release. Windows remains the primary authoritative local release build; macOS and Linux can be built in GitHub Actions or locally when needed.

### Impact

Release automation and operator workflow become path-aware. The project avoids paying binary rebuild cost on ordinary docs/site/eval commits while still enforcing executable rebuilds for real CLI changes.

## gad-135: GAD distribution is GitHub-first: executable releases + skills.sh, not npm publish

### Summary

GAD should not rely on public npm publication as the primary distribution path. The official install story is: (1) download a GitHub Release executable for the gad operational CLI, and (2) install the published GAD skills from the GitHub repo via skills.sh / `npx skills add`. Users who already have Node may optionally install or run the CLI directly from the GitHub repo, but that is a secondary convenience path, not the canonical onboarding. Existing docs that assume `npx get-anything-done@latest` from the public npm registry are now stale.

### Impact

Quick start, install docs, and release messaging must move from npm-first to GitHub-first. Release assets target standalone executables. Skill installation instructions point at skills.sh + GitHub repo installs.

## gad-134: Site reusability review needed â€” cut down on duplicate edits

### Summary

The site needs a reusability pass: shared patterns reimplemented across pages, duplicate components, data shape changes requiring edits everywhere. Pipeline-generated data should drive generic components. Queued for future session.

### Impact

Reduces maintenance burden as eval count grows to hundreds.

## gad-133: Methodology must be tied to a buildable project with artifacts

### Summary

Even methodology-only tracks must implement a buildable project. No catalog entry without a concrete eval project and evaluation path. If methodology produces signals (pressure, compliance, traces), artifacts must ship. GAD-on-GAD is the reference. Vibes-only theory is not a catalog entry.

### Impact

Project market listing requirement: must have build or artifact set.

## gad-132: Projects have evals, evals have rounds, rounds triggered by requirements changes

### Summary

Conceptual hierarchy: Project â†’ eval projects â†’ rounds. A round is triggered when requirements change. Within a round, requirements can iterate without triggering a new round until new evals branch or the contract fundamentally changes. Per-project round windows for display filtering.

### Impact

Data model clarification. Per-project round timelines on site. CLI round numbering per-project.

## gad-131: Rubric belongs on project detail and eval pages, not standalone /rubric

### Summary

The /rubric page content should live on project detail pages and eval run pages where it's contextual. A standalone /rubric page may remain as a methodology reference but the primary rubric experience is per-project and per-run.

### Impact

Rubric display on project detail + run pages. /rubric becomes secondary reference.

## gad-130: /project-market â€” dedicated catalog page for all eval projects

### Summary

Ship /project-market as the dedicated catalog page listing ALL eval projects (not just ETD + explainer). ETD and explainer are featured/canonical (pinned, badged) but not exclusive. Default filter: last 5 rounds per project (not global). Home page playable archive shrinks to a teaser linking to /project-market. Full handoff spec committed as PROJECT-MARKET-SPEC.md. Projects own rounds. Requirements change â†’ new round. In-round edits allowed until new evals branch.

### Impact

New /project-market route. Home page lightened. All 30+ eval projects browsable with default filtering. Featured canonical projects visible.

## gad-129: Official build/release pipeline for GAD CLI â€” Windows first, publish ourselves

### Summary

Ship an official build/release pipeline for the GAD CLI. Windows first (build on this machine, publish ourselves). Mac and Linux can use GitHub workflows. Users install via npx get-anything-done or a published binary. The CLI should let users set up blank projects or eval projects. The /quickstart flow: install coding agent CLI â†’ install GAD â†’ set up project or eval â†’ run.

### Impact

New release pipeline. Windows binary published manually. Mac/Linux via GitHub Actions. npx install flow verified end-to-end.

## gad-128: GAD CLI is an operational CLI â€” distinct from coding agent CLIs

### Summary

The GAD CLI (gad) is an operational CLI for the GAD framework and ecosystem. It is NOT a coding agent CLI like Claude Code CLI or Codex CLI. Those are coding agent runtimes. GAD provides commands, agents, skills, and artifacts FOR those coding agent CLIs. The site must make this distinction clear â€” framework + catalog on the same page, with the GAD CLI described as operational tooling that agents and users invoke, not the agent itself. The /gad page skill bootstrap sets should show the various hypotheses and what artifacts/tools are available per hypothesis condition.

### Impact

Site /gad page redesigned. Framework + catalog merged. Clear copy distinguishing operational CLI from coding agent CLIs. Build/release pipeline for the GAD CLI as a distributable.

## gad-125: ID format: per-project namespace + type prefix + number

### Summary

IDs encode project, artifact type, and number. Format: PROJECT-TYPE-NUMBER. Examples: GAD-D-119 (GAD decision 119), GAD-T-22-01 (GAD task phase 22 task 01), GAD-P-22 (GAD phase 22), ETD-T-3-01 (escape-the-dungeon task). Type codes: D=decision, T=task, P=phase, R=requirement, B=bug, S=skill. Namespace is per-project (GAD, ETD, GRIME, etc.) not always "GAD" â€” GAD is the project name, not a framework prefix. New IDs use this format; old IDs stay until a migration script handles them as a batch. On the site: each segment color-coded (namespace=accent, type=type-color, number=foreground). HoverCard on every ID shows full artifact details with segment breakdown (e.g., "22 = phase, 01 = task"). Traceability over brevity â€” full IDs everywhere, no abbreviations in cross-references.

### Impact

New IDs in DECISIONS.xml, TASK-REGISTRY.xml use new format going forward. CLI output shows new format. Site Ref component gains HoverCard + color-coded segments. Migration script (gad migrate-ids) for batch conversion when ready.

## gad-124: Playable archive becomes a dedicated /evals page â€” catalog/marketplace pattern

### Summary

The current playable archive on the landing page works for showing the last 5 rounds of game builds. But we need a dedicated /evals page that shows ALL evals across ALL rounds as a catalog/marketplace. Filter by domain, tech stack, round, workflow, review status. Show build size + source size. Show skill provenance. Show eval relationships (e.g., reverse-engineer â†’ build-from-requirements). Everything is free â€” no paywalling. The landing page keeps a compact "latest" view linking to the full /evals page.

### Impact

New /evals route. Landing page playable archive becomes a compact preview. Full catalog on dedicated page.

## gad-123: Docs must be on the site â€” docs/ markdown files need site routes

### Summary

Documentation in docs/ (quick-start.md, eval-guide.md) must be rendered as routes on the site, not just exist as markdown files in the repo. The site is the primary documentation surface. Docs routes: /quickstart, /eval-guide. These should be built from the markdown files during prebuild, same as other content.

### Impact

New site routes. Build pipeline renders docs/*.md to site pages.

## gad-122: gad self-eval CLI command for pressure and framework metrics

### Summary

Add `gad self-eval` CLI command that runs the compute-self-eval pipeline and prints findings. Pipeline scripts should have corresponding CLI commands so agents and users can invoke them. `gad self-eval` runs compute-self-eval.mjs and outputs: pressure per phase, overhead ratio, loop compliance, high-pressure phases. This is the CLI-first principle from gad-99.

### Impact

New CLI command. Replaces needing to run node scripts/compute-self-eval.mjs manually.

## gad-121: Rounds are per-project, defined by requirements version changes

### Summary

A "round" for a project is defined when its requirements document changes version. Each eval project has its own round timeline, not a single global round number. The current global rounds (1-5) are specific to the escape-the-dungeon family. Other projects (reverse-engineer, skill-evaluation-app, etc.) have their own round 1 when they first run. Round number is derived from the requirements_version field progression in TRACE.json across runs.

### Impact

gad rounds gains --project flag for per-project rounds. Site shows per-project round timelines. Global rounds remain as the escape-the-dungeon view for backward compatibility.

## gad-120: Skill provenance tags: installed, inherited, authored, 3rd-party, gad-framework

### Summary

Skills in an eval run have provenance: (1) installed â€” put in template via --install-skills before the run, (2) inherited â€” copied from a prior run via inherit-skills, (3) authored â€” created by the agent during the run (didn't exist at start), (4) 3rd-party â€” from external sources (npx skills), (5) gad-framework â€” official GAD skills. Provenance is tracked in .installed-skills.json and diffed against the post-run skill directory to identify authored skills. The site displays provenance tags on skill badges. GAD framework skills have a formal visual treatment distinct from inherited/authored.

### Impact

Eval TRACE.json gains skills_provenance section. Site shows provenance badges. inherit-skills gains --latest-only flag option.

## gad-119: Interactive Gantt chart for phases and rounds with hover stats

### Summary

Replace or augment the current phase/round displays with interactive Gantt charts. Phases as horizontal bars on a timeline. Click a phase to expand tasks. Hover for stats (pressure score, token count, task count, duration). Same pattern for RoundResults â€” Gantt chart of rounds, click shows results, hover shows summary stats. Use recharts or a lightweight Gantt library compatible with the existing site stack.

### Impact

New component: GanttChart in components/charts/. Used in both Workflow section (phases) and RoundResults section (rounds). Pressure data feeds the visualization.

## gad-118: GAD discipline scoring for evals using the framework

### Summary

Evals running with the GAD workflow should compute GAD discipline scores from their traces â€” same metrics as self-eval (overhead ratio, loop compliance, planning doc freshness, decision capture rate). These scores live in the eval's TRACE.json and are displayed on the run detail page. The eval's source files retain trace data for future reference. README of each eval repo should include scores when reviewed. CLI automates score injection into READMEs.

### Impact

Eval TRACE.json gains gad_discipline section. CLI command to inject scores into README. Site displays discipline scores alongside quality scores for GAD workflow evals.

## gad-117: Quick start guide + simplified install â€” no repo clone required

### Summary

GAD needs a quick start guide for onboarding: install Claude Code or Codex CLI, install GAD (`npx get-anything-done`), run first eval. No repo clone required for users â€” the eval framework should work from any project. Documentation priority: quick start, eval setup guide, skill creation guide, CLI reference.

### Impact

New docs: quick-start.md, eval-guide.md. Install flow simplified. Site gets /quickstart route.

## gad-116: Playable archive redesign for thousands of evals across domains

### Summary

The Playable Archive needs redesign for scale â€” thousands of evaluations across game/skill/software/business/stories domains, with relationships between evals (e.g., reverse-engineer â†’ build-from-requirements). Non-GUI builds show their README. Multi-domain filtering. Tech stack badges. Source + build size display. Related eval linking. This is a Phase 28+ project.

### Impact

Major site redesign queued. Current playable archive works for dozens of game builds but won't scale to thousands of multi-domain evals.

## gad-115: Pressure per phase drives skill creation opportunities

### Summary

When pressure is high on a phase (many tasks, many crosscuts per gad-75/gad-79), that signals skill creation opportunities â€” the complexity required more work, meaning patterns exist that could be captured as skills. After completing a high-pressure phase, review for: repeated commands (â†’ script/CLI), repeated patterns (â†’ skill), repeated coordination (â†’ agent). Pressure scores should be computed per phase and displayed on the site with interactive visualization. The Gantt chart idea: phases as horizontal bars, click for tasks, hover for stats (pressure, tokens, task count).

### Impact

Pressure computed per phase in the self-eval pipeline. Site gets interactive Gantt chart component for phases and rounds. High-pressure phases flagged for skill creation review.

## gad-114: Human time estimation vs agent time â€” comparison metric per eval

### Summary

Before each eval run, the agent estimates how long the requirements would take a human developer to implement (without AI tools). This goes into TRACE.json as `human_estimate_hours`. After the run, we have actual agent duration. The ratio (estimate / actual) shows the speedup factor. This is publishable data â€” "this eval's requirements would take a human ~40 hours, the agent did it in 23 minutes." Time estimation is part of the eval prompt, not a separate step.

### Impact

TRACE.json gains human_estimate_hours. Eval prompt includes estimation instruction. Site shows speedup ratio. Powerful marketing data.

## gad-113: Evals have timestamps, build requirements, and completion gates

### Summary

Every eval run is timestamped (started, ended) in TRACE.json.
