An eval is only "done" when: (1) the build requirement is produced (game build, requirements file, composition, running app), (2) human review is submitted. Build requirement is defined per domain in gad.json as `build_requirement` field. For reverse-engineer, the build is the requirements file â€” but it's gated until a second eval implements those requirements and produces a working build. For video evals, the build is the Remotion composition (src is the build until mp4 export exists). Eval data includes source file sizes alongside build sizes.

### Impact

gad.json gains build_requirement field. TRACE.json gains source_size_bytes and build_size_bytes. Eval completion is gated on build + review. Site shows both sizes.

## gad-112: gad eval inherit-skills â€” CLI command to copy emergent skills from one eval into another

### Summary

Easy CLI command to inherit skills from a completed eval run into a new eval template. `gad eval inherit-skills --from escape-the-dungeon-bare/v5 --to escape-the-dungeon-emergent`. This is the mechanism for testing GAD+Skills (with inheritance). The command copies skills from the source run's game/.planning/skills/ into the target template, records the lineage in metadata. Distinct from --install-skills which installs framework skills â€” this inherits agent-authored runtime skills.

### Impact

New CLI command. Enables easy emergent skill testing. Skills lineage tracked in eval metadata.

## gad-111: Tech stack comparison evals: same requirements, different frameworks

### Summary

For game evals, compare tech stacks: Phaser, PixiJS, Three.js, Babylon.js alongside existing Kaplay/DOM builds. Same requirements, different stack in the template. The tech stack is metadata in gad.json (`tech_stack` field). Eval matrix: requirements with tech stack specified, requirements without tech stack (agent chooses), requirements with skills, requirements with skills + tech stack. This produces data on whether framework choice or skill availability matters more for output quality.

### Impact

New eval projects per tech stack. gad.json gains tech_stack field. Eval comparison includes tech stack as a variable. Site shows tech stack badges on eval cards.

## gad-110: Eval domain categories: game, skill, software, business, stories

### Summary

Eval projects are categorized by domain â€” not just mode (greenfield/brownfield) and workflow (gad/bare/emergent). Domains: game (escape-the-dungeon, tech-stack comparisons), skill (reverse-engineer, gad-skill-creator), software (business apps, CMS templates), business (productivity tools), stories (narrative content, compositions). The domain determines the build requirement â€” games need a playable build, skills need a requirements file, software needs a running app, stories need a composition. Domain is a field in gad.json.

### Impact

gad.json gains `domain` field. gad eval list shows domain column. Site groups evals by domain. Playable archive becomes multi-domain (games + software + readmes).

## gad-109: gad data uses lowdb with /data as the managed database

### Summary

The gad data CLI subcommand uses lowdb (https://github.com/typicode/lowdb) with the data/ folder as the database. JSON files in data/ are both the database AND version-controlled codebase artifacts â€” lowdb reads/writes them directly. This gives us CRUD operations via CLI while keeping the data human-readable and git-tracked. Implement after eval --install-skills ships.

### Impact

Add lowdb as a dependency. data/*.json files gain programmatic CRUD via gad data. Still human-editable and git-tracked.

## gad-108: Pipeline is a formal artifact category alongside skill, agent, eval

### Summary

Pipeline tasks (compute-self-eval, build-site-data, etc.) are a distinct category in our artifact tracking. The table format with Type column (skill, eval, pipeline) is the standard for listing artifacts. Pipeline tasks should be tagged in TASK-REGISTRY.xml same as skills and agents. Decoupled piping with the GAD CLI is the design goal â€” pipelines are composable CLI command chains.

### Impact

TASK-REGISTRY.xml gains type="pipeline" alongside type="skill" and type="agent". Artifact tables include Pipeline as a category.

## gad-107: gad eval --input for skill installation into eval projects

### Summary

The eval CLI should support installing skills into eval project templates before or after runs. `gad eval run --install-skills path/to/skill [...]` installs skill(s) into the eval template, populates the data (AGENTS.md skill references, template/skills/ directory), and tracks what was installed. This enables: (1) point at any skill folder and wrap it as an eval, (2) install skills into existing eval projects that already have source from prior runs, (3) run evaluations with vs without specific skills. The CLI handles the automation of copying skills, updating AGENTS.md, and recording the installation in TRACE.json. Works with npx skills format, local paths, and GitHub URLs.

### Impact

New eval CLI flag. Enables skill evaluation without manual template editing. Tracks skill installations as eval metadata. Prioritize before gad data CLI.

## gad-106: Defer gad data CLI â€” keep it light

### Summary

The gad data CRUD subcommand (gad-101) is deferred. Keep the CLI light. JSON files are readable and editable directly. Add data commands only when there's a proven workflow need, not speculatively.

### Impact

gad-101 deprioritized. Focus CLI work on eval and rounds commands instead.

## gad-105: Enhance workflow: review task, find repetitive commands, propose script/CLI/skill

### Summary

Add "enhance" as a workflow alongside merge. When reviewing a completed task: (1) what repetitive commands were there? (2) should we create a script and invoke that? (3) or stack commands and keep instructions? (4) or officially add to the gad CLI because it's workflow-related? This is the feedback loop from usage to tooling. A task that used 5 sequential Bash commands is a candidate for a script. A task that chained 3 gad CLI calls is a candidate for a new composite command. Enhance doesn't replace merge â€” merge handles overlapping skills, enhance handles graduating patterns from tasks into reusable tooling.

### Impact

New workflow step after task completion: enhance review. Feeds the CLI-grows â†’ skills-thin cycle from gad-99.

## gad-104: Task-skill-agent attribution is a formal tag in GAD artifacts

### Summary

Every task in TASK-REGISTRY.xml should tag which skill(s) and agent(s) were involved. Format: `skill="skill-name"` and `agent="agent-name"` attributes on the task element. Named agents (gad-planner, gad-verifier, etc.) use their name; unnamed/default agents use "default". Multiple skills on one task signals a merge/enhance opportunity. The AI knows during the session which skills and agents are active â€” capture it in artifacts since we can't always rely on trace logs. Standard naming convention follows existing GAD patterns: lowercase-kebab-case for skills/agents.

### Impact

TASK-REGISTRY.xml tasks gain skill="" and agent="" attributes. Planning doc updates include attribution. Enables querying "which skills were used for which tasks" from artifacts alone without trace logs.

## gad-103: Computed trace metrics are long-lived â€” raw logs are capped

### Summary

Raw trace logs (.gad-log/*.jsonl, .trace-events.jsonl) have caps (1000 events for trace, daily rotation for gad-log). But computed/mined values from those logs are long-lived static data â€” once computed, they persist in site/data/self-eval.json and don't need recomputation. The compute-self-eval pipeline runs during prebuild and produces snapshots. Key metrics to mine per computation window: tokens used per phase, tokens for the whole project thus far, number of tasks completed, pressure score (per gad-75/gad-79 definitions), skill invocation counts, framework overhead ratio, loop compliance. These are expensive to compute from raw logs so we gather everything we can during the computation window and store the results permanently.

### Impact

self-eval.json grows over time as an append-only metrics store. Each prebuild adds a new snapshot. Raw logs can rotate freely because the computed values survive independently. The pipeline needs to capture per-phase token counts and pressure scores in addition to current metrics.

## gad-102: Skill creation immediately produces an eval project on the site

### Summary

When a skill is created via gad:create-skill, immediately scaffold an eval project for it (`gad eval setup`) and register it on the site. GAD framework skills (skills/commands/agents related to the framework itself) must be labeled as framework-level and require formal evaluation. Non-framework skills used in real workflow still show usage data on the site from trace logs â€” the data is already being collected. The distinction: framework skills get formal controlled evals, all skills get observational usage data from real work.

### Impact

gad:create-skill workflow now includes `gad eval setup --project <skill-name>` as a mandatory step. Site shows two data types: formal eval scores (controlled) and real-workflow usage metrics (observational). Framework skills are tagged for identification.

## gad-101: gad data â€” CRUD subcommand for site/data/ and data/ JSON pseudo-database

### Summary

Add `gad data list|get|set|add|remove` subcommand for CRUD operations on the JSON files in data/ (repo-level pseudo-db) and site/data/ (site-level static data). Treat it like a local database with CLI access. Examples: `gad data list` shows all collections, `gad data get hypotheses.freedom` reads a specific entry, `gad data set rounds."Round 5".status awaiting_review` updates a field. This replaces manual JSON editing and makes data operations traceable in the log.

### Impact

New CLI subcommand. Skills and agents use `gad data` instead of reading/writing JSON directly. All data mutations are logged, making self-eval metrics more accurate.

## gad-100: GAD-specific skill creator built from Anthropic's skill-creator + our artifacts/CLI

### Summary

Install the Anthropic skill-creator (`npx skills add https://github.com/anthropics/skills --skill skill-creator`) and use it to create a GAD-specific derivative. The GAD skill creator knows our planning artifacts (STATE.xml, TASK-REGISTRY.xml, DECISIONS.xml), our CLI commands, our eval framework, and the agentskills.io conventions. It doesn't create more skills for our artifacts â€” it creates skills tailored to how we work WITH our artifacts and CLI. When 3 sequential CLI commands solve a workflow, the GAD skill creator recognizes that pattern and produces a skill that chains them. merge-skill must reference agentskills.io docs as professional guidance during merges.

### Impact

New skill: gad-skill-creator (in .agents/skills/). Built using the Anthropic skill-creator's references and methodology. merge-skill updated to reference agentskills.io. Skills become lighter as CLI grows.

## gad-99: Skills prefer CLI commands but use good judgment â€” methodology skills are valid

### Summary

Skills should use CLI commands when they exist â€” `gad eval setup` not manual file creation. But this is judgment, not a rule. Toolless methodology skills are perfectly valid. The goal is to pack utility into the CLI and have skills leverage it, creating a cycle: CLI grows â†’ skills chain commands â†’ overlapping skills get merged â†’ CLI absorbs proven patterns. When 3 sequential commands solve a workflow, that's a skill. But pure instructional skills (like self-eval rubrics or debugging methodology) don't need tooling to be useful or evaluable â€” we evaluate through rounds.

### Impact

CLI and skills co-evolve. Neither is mandatory. Good judgment about when to use tooling vs methodology. Every new CLI command should be checked against existing skills for replacement opportunities.

## gad-98: Remove /gad:join-discord â€” dead command

### Summary

The /gad:join-discord command is not useful. Remove it from the help reference and skill registry.

### Impact

One fewer command in the help output. No workflow impact.

## gad-97: Skills that measure GAD are framework-level, not private

### Summary

trace-analysis and self-eval are public framework skills in .agents/skills/ â€” they measure GAD itself, which is useful to any GAD user. Private project-local skills (.claude/private-skills/) are reserved for truly project-specific workflow automation that has no value outside the specific repo. Skills don't need tooling to be evaluated â€” they can be evaluated through rounds by observing whether agents use them effectively. The "skill invocation gap" in hook traces is a methodology observation, not a bug to fix.

### Impact

trace-analysis and self-eval moved to .agents/skills/. The private tier (.claude/private-skills/) remains available but only for genuinely project-specific automation. Skill evaluation happens through rounds, not through mandated tooling.

## gad-96: Reverse-engineer eval: requirements quality measured by build success

### Summary

The reverse-engineer skill produces requirements from an existing codebase. The only way to know if those requirements are good is to build from them â€” same methodology as game evals. Eval flow: reverse-engineer a project â†’ agent builds from the generated requirements â†’ score the result against the original. This is a new eval project (reverse-engineer-eval) with its own scoring rubric focused on requirements completeness and fidelity. The reverse-engineer skill should work in conjunction with map-codebase â€” map gives structural understanding, reverse-engineer distills actionable requirements. If the target has GAD planning docs, those should be consumed as additional signal.

### Impact

New eval project: reverse-engineer-eval. New skill tooling: reverse-engineer + map-codebase integration. The eval for reverse-engineer is inherently two-phase (produce requirements, then build from them) which makes it the first eval that tests a GAD skill rather than a game implementation workflow.

## gad-95: GAD self-evaluation â€” real-world brownfield data from building GAD itself

### Summary

Building GAD + the site IS a GAD project. We have 7 GAD workspaces in this monorepo, 2320+ tool call events across 4 days, 1000+ trace events, 93 decisions, 141 done tasks. This is real brownfield data â€” not isolated game evals. We should be scoring ourselves per "round" of GAD framework development: how well did GAD help us build GAD? Per-round metrics from the trace data (tool mix, commit rhythm, context compactions, skill invocations) become the first brownfield eval that isn't escape-the-dungeon. Every GAD project in the monorepo (grime-time, repub-builder, mb-cli-framework, etc.) is potential eval data for the framework.

### Impact

New data source for the site: real-world GAD usage metrics alongside the controlled game evals. The landing page should show both: "here's how 3 workflows compare on a game" AND "here's how GAD performs on real multi-project work." This is the most credible evidence for or against GAD because there's no cherry-picking â€” it's our actual work.

## gad-94: .agents/ convention extended â€” agents/ and commands/ should follow the same cross-client pattern as skills/

### Summary

User observation 2026-04-10: "if we are canonically putting skills in our .agents folder and checking in that repo, then should we not do the same for agents and commands?" Currently: skills live at .agents/skills/ (agentskills.io convention per gad-80), agents live at agents/ (repo root), commands live at commands/gad/ (repo root). The inconsistency means install.js has different copy paths for each artifact type. Policy: (1) Long-term, agents and commands should also live under .agents/ for consistency â€” .agents/agents/ and .agents/commands/. (2) Short-term, this is NOT a blocking migration â€” the agentskills.io standard only covers skills, not agents or commands. Agents have a different loading mechanism (they're not progressive-disclosure catalog items, they're spawn targets). Commands are Claude-Code-specific slash commands. (3) When we migrate: git mv agents/ .agents/agents/, git mv commands/gad/ .agents/commands/, update install.js and prebuild scanners. Queued as a follow-up â€” not done this turn. (4) The user's broader point is about consistency and discoverability. A new user opening the repo should find everything agent-related under one tree (.agents/) rather than scattered across root directories.

### Impact

(1) Queued migration: agents/ â†’ .agents/agents/, commands/gad/ â†’ .agents/commands/. Not blocking. (2) install.js already handles both separately â€” the migration is a path change, not a logic change. (3) The prebuild scans agents/ and commands/gad/ with their own loops â€” update the constants when migrating. (4) This does NOT affect how Claude Code or other runtimes discover agents â€” they load from .claude/agents/ or similar runtime-specific paths. The .agents/ convention is the SOURCE location in the repo, not the runtime location.

## gad-93: Agent-parallelization hypothesis â€” does multi-agent work improve outcomes or just burn tokens?

### Summary

User question 2026-04-10: "we would want to test if putting [agents] in and parallelizing the work specifically speed up development at the least, use more tokens vs less tokens if we didn't use multiple agents." New research hypothesis: the GSD-inherited multi-agent model (18 specialized agents with distinct roles â€” researcher, planner, executor, verifier, debugger, etc.) may improve development speed or quality by parallelizing work. BUT it may also increase token consumption without proportional quality improvement. The GAD framework already ships 21 gad-* agents in the agents/ directory â€” they're available but their impact on eval outcomes has never been measured. We need a test condition where agents are explicitly spawned as subagents (via Claude Code's Agent tool or Codex's parallel-task mechanism) vs a control where the same work is done serially by a single agent. Metrics to capture: total_tokens, duration_ms, tool_use_count, subagent_spawn_count (already in trace v4 schema via gad-50), and human_review quality score. The hypothesis is falsified if multi-agent runs use significantly more tokens for the same or lower quality. It is confirmed if multi-agent runs are faster AND at least equal quality. This is a new line on the hypothesis tracks chart.

### Impact

(1) New eval condition queued for future rounds: "escape-the-dungeon-multi-agent" â€” same requirements, same workflow (GAD), but the AGENTS.md explicitly instructs the agent to spawn subagents for parallelizable tasks (e.g. planning and implementation phases run as separate subagents). (2) Metrics already capturable: subagent_spawn_count in TRACE.json from trace v4 hooks. token comparison is the key signal. (3) This ties to the broader question of whether the GSD upstream's 18-agent model (gsd-project-researcher, gsd-planner, gsd-executor, etc.) was beneficial or overhead. GAD inherited the agents but hasn't tested whether they help. (4) The hypothesis tracks chart gains a new dashed line (multi-agent, planned). (5) The eval template matrix gains a new column for the multi-agent condition: same as GAD but with subagent-spawn instructions in AGENTS.md.

## gad-92: docs/ is GSD upstream â€” archive it, don't rewrite it

### Summary

User review 2026-04-10: "these docs do not describe gad, they describe GSD, which GAD is based off of, this is a fork of GSD." The docs/ directory (62 files referencing GSD across ARCHITECTURE.md, AGENTS.md, COMMANDS.md, CLI-TOOLS.md, CONFIGURATION.md, USER-GUIDE.md, FEATURES.md, context-budget.md, context-monitor.md, workflow-discuss-mode.md, superpowers/, and 4 language translations) is GSD upstream documentation that shipped with the fork. NONE of it describes GAD. Policy: (1) Move docs/ to docs-gsd-upstream/ as an explicit archive with a README noting it's prior art from the upstream fork, not GAD documentation. (2) GAD's documentation is the SITE (get-anything-done.vercel.app) â€” /methodology, /standards, /decisions, /glossary, /contribute, /skeptic, etc. We do NOT maintain a parallel docs/ folder. (3) The GSD docs are valuable as reference for what the upstream was doing (cited on /lineage), and some content may inspire future GAD features â€” but we evaluate those features through our eval framework rather than inherit GSD's claims. (4) The GSD agent patterns (18 specialized gsd-* agents described in docs/AGENTS.md) are useful prior art for the agent-parallelization hypothesis (gad-93).

### Impact

(1) Quick rename: git mv docs/ docs-gsd-upstream/. Add a README.md at the top explaining the provenance. (2) Any site page currently linking to docs/ files updates to docs-gsd-upstream/. (3) The prebuild does NOT scan docs/ for anything â€” confirmed by grep. (4) No site content changes needed â€” the site is self-contained.

## gad-91: CLI-as-a-variable â€” GAD skills that use the CLI need the CLI installed in the eval template

### Summary

User observation 2026-04-10: "are we including the CLI in these templates. if the gad skills are using the gad cli, then that must also be used to fully implement GAD. just using GAD artifacts is one thing also, adding the CLI and snapshot is something I want to test as well." This is correct: several GAD skills (execute-phase, plan-phase, verify-phase, task-checkpoint, snapshot-optimize) invoke `gad` CLI commands like `gad snapshot`, `gad state`, `gad tasks`. If those skills are in the template but the CLI is not installed, the skills fail silently. The CLI is CURRENTLY available in eval worktrees (because the worktree is a git checkout of the same repo that contains `bin/gad.cjs`), but it's not DOCUMENTED as a deliberate inclusion. Policy: (1) For the GAD and GAD+Emergent conditions, the CLI is explicitly allowed and skills may invoke it. (2) For the Planning-only condition, the CLI is NOT included â€” the agent has the XML artifacts but not the tooling. This tests whether the artifacts alone help. (3) For Bare and Emergent conditions, the CLI is NOT available. (4) A future 6th condition â€” "CLI-only" (CLI + snapshot + no skills, no planning artifacts) â€” could test whether just the CLI loop (`gad snapshot` â†’ `gad state` â†’ work â†’ commit) helps without any of the planning XML or skill methodology. Queued as a possible round-6 addition.

### Impact

(1) GAD template's AGENTS.md should explicitly tell the agent to use `gad snapshot` and `gad state` commands. (2) Planning-only template's AGENTS.md should NOT mention the CLI. (3) The template matrix table on /methodology gains a new row: "GAD CLI available". (4) Future 6th condition (CLI-only) deferred to planning â€” not scaffolded yet. (5) The existing eval worktrees already have the CLI available via `bin/gad.cjs` in the git checkout, so no file-copy change is needed â€” just documentation clarity about what's allowed per condition.

## gad-90: Greenfield â†’ brownfield lineage model â€” branching visualization + same 5 hypotheses retested

### Summary

User directive 2026-04-10: "we want to show how we take the source from a greenfield and then run some brownfield evaluations on it. when we do that we want to represent that as branching like git branching or mermaid graphs." Formalizes the greenfield-to-brownfield relationship: (1) Every brownfield eval project references a specific greenfield run as its baseline via `gad.json baseline: { project, version, source }`. The source path points at the preserved run's code directory. (2) The site's hypothesis-tracks chart must distinguish greenfield from brownfield â€” either as separate charts, a toggle, or a layered view. Greenfield rounds are the trunk. Brownfield rounds branch off from a specific greenfield run's output. (3) The same 5-condition matrix (bare / planning-only / GAD / emergent / GAD+emergent) applies to brownfield work â€” testing the same hypotheses but for CODE EXTENSION instead of greenfield creation. This is the generalization axis the freedom hypothesis needs: maybe bare wins greenfield but GAD wins brownfield (where planning helps navigate an existing codebase). (4) Brownfield eval runs are associated with their greenfield predecessor per domain. The branching visualization shows this lineage: "Bare v3 (greenfield, round 3, 0.70) â†’ branches into Brownfield-Bare v1, Brownfield-Emergent v1, Brownfield-GAD v1." (5) Any greenfield run can become a brownfield baseline â€” not just the best-scoring one. When the user selects a round on the hypothesis chart, the brownfield branches from that round should be visible.

### Impact

(1) The existing brownfield eval projects (etd-brownfield-bare, etd-brownfield-emergent, etd-brownfield-gad) already reference bare v3 as baseline â€” the data model works. (2) New site component: EvalLineageGraph rendering a directed graph (greenfield runs as nodes â†’ brownfield runs as branches). Could use mermaid-js, a custom SVG, or recharts tree. (3) The hypothesis-tracks chart needs a greenfield/brownfield mode selector â€” when viewing greenfield, it shows the existing line chart; when viewing brownfield, it shows the branching results. (4) For round 5: after the 5 greenfield conditions run, the best-scoring greenfield run(s) become the baseline for round 5's brownfield conditions. This means brownfield round 5 DEPENDS on greenfield round 5 completing first. (5) The 5-condition brownfield matrix is: brownfield-bare (no skills, extend existing code), brownfield-planning-only (planning XML, extend code), brownfield-GAD (full framework, extend code), brownfield-emergent (inherited skills, extend code), brownfield-GAD-emergent (everything, extend code). Some of these don't exist yet as projects â€” scaffold them when greenfield round 5 results are in. (6) /roadmap page gains a mermaid-style graph showing the lineage across rounds.

## gad-88: New eval domain â€” GUI-for-requirements-and-harness (skill-evaluation-app)

### Summary

User idea 2026-04-09: "lets make a GUI with requirements for it and put it a part of our rounds to test our theories. which I still see an emergent page, but not a hypothesis page with graphs and etc detailing it out visually." Two nested ideas here: (A) add more visualizations to /hypotheses, (B) create a NEW eval project where the agent's task is to build a browser-based GUI for authoring requirements and running the per-skill eval harness. The latter is a new eval domain â€” distinct from escape-the-dungeon (game dev) and gad-explainer-video (video composition). Testing the same hypotheses (freedom / CSH / emergent-evolution) on a THIRD task domain: front-end application development with a defined functional spec. If the freedom hypothesis still holds on GUI development, the hypothesis generalizes beyond creative implementation. If the compound-skills hypothesis still holds, the skill evolution effect is task-domain-independent. This becomes a major signal for generalization, which per the SKEPTIC cross-cutting critique #5 is the single biggest methodology hole in the project. The GUI itself, once built, also becomes OUR tool â€” we author requirements in it, we run evals through it, we view benchmarks through it. Dogfood opportunity: eval project that, when finished, is part of the eval framework. Scaffolding queued as task 22-51. Rubric dimensions proposed: ui_usability (0.30), requirements_ergonomics (0.20), harness_integration (0.20), visualization_quality (0.15), stability (0.15).

### Impact

(1) New eval project scaffolded at evals/skill-evaluation-app/ this turn with gad.json + REQUIREMENTS.md. (2) The project has the same three-condition structure as escape-the-dungeon: skill-evaluation-app (GAD framework), skill-evaluation-app-bare (no framework), skill-evaluation-app-emergent (inherits skills from prior runs). Follow-up task: create the bare + emergent siblings. (3) Round 5+ includes this eval domain as the generalization test â€” specifically, once escape-the-dungeon v5 runs AND gad-explainer-video runs, the third track activates. (4) Round planning gets more complex â€” /roadmap page needs to render multiple eval domains per round, not just escape-the-dungeon. (5) /hypotheses gets concrete test-domain chips under each hypothesis card showing which domains have tested it. (6) The freedom hypothesis evidence now has a clearly-defined falsification condition: if bare fails on skill-evaluation-app or gad-explainer-video while GAD succeeds, freedom is falsified for those domains.

## gad-87: Per-skill eval harness â€” GAD-native, not skill-creator's Python harness

### Summary

User directive 2026-04-09: "also note on the skill-creator and its eval script with python. it didnt work for gad's workflow methodology. claude gave a 4/8 tasks 2 times and I think it was testing tool calls. anyway we want to avoid that and have actionable testable frameworks." The anthropic skill-creator ships a Python eval harness (`eval-viewer/generate_review.py` + `scripts/aggregate_benchmark`) that spawns parallel subagent runs with_skill vs without_skill, grades assertions via `agents/grader.md`, and emits `benchmark.json` + an HTML viewer. The user tried it for GAD's workflow methodology and got inconsistent results (4/8 tasks, twice). Diagnosis: it appears to test tool calls, not workflow adherence. GAD's evaluation question is different â€” we care whether a skill changes agent behavior on a concrete task (e.g., "did the agent actually follow the session-discipline checklist"), not whether a tool was called. Policy: GAD builds its own per-skill eval harness using the agentskills.io evals/evals.json + with_skill/without_skill + benchmark.json format (so our skills can still be evaluated by skill-creator compatible tools if desired) but with a grading strategy tuned to workflow adherence: assertions explicitly target behavioral outcomes (file mutations, commit subjects, task-registry updates, trace events emitted) rather than tool calls. Harness is a new `gad eval skill <name>` subcommand that (1) reads evals/evals.json inside the skill dir, (2) spawns two clean-context subagent runs per test case (with_skill + without_skill) via the existing `gad worktree` mechanism, (3) captures trace events from our v4 schema (gad-50) as the observable signal, (4) grades assertions by querying trace events + file mutations + commit log instead of LLM self-report, (5) emits a benchmark.json compatible with the agentskills.io format but populated from deterministic trace evidence.

### Impact

(1) New task queued: build `gad eval skill <name>` CLI subcommand. High priority â€” this is how every GAD skill graduates from experimental to canonical per gad-86. (2) Every skill in .agents/skills/ gets an evals/evals.json starter template. First candidates: create-skill (easiest to test â€” "did the agent author a SKILL.md with valid frontmatter?"), session-discipline (testable via: did the agent run gad snapshot, did it update TASK-REGISTRY.xml, did it commit with a task-id prefix?), portfolio-sync (testable via: did the agent run build-site-data.mjs, did it regenerate the generated TS, did the git commit subject match the portfolio-sync convention?). (3) The harness becomes the concrete answer to programmatic-eval GAP G2 (skill-trigger coverage) AND G11 (skill-inheritance hygiene) â€” once we can run it, we have the automated skill-effectiveness signal gad-69 has been asking for since the programmatic-eval audit. (4) This is the load-bearing piece that turns the skill-count policy (gad-81) into an enforceable system: prune by graduation status, merge experimental duplicates via merge-skill, require benchmarks for any skill that wants to be canonical.

## gad-86: Skill graduation criterion â€” no evaluation = experimental

### Summary

User answer 2026-04-09 to discussion Q2: "they become canonical if they become useful, right now skills that dont have evaluations are considered experimental. also emergent skills stay in the gad repo forever." Formalizes the skill lifecycle: (1) A skill with no evals/evals.json + no benchmark run against it is EXPERIMENTAL, regardless of origin. (2) A skill with a passing with_skill-vs-without_skill benchmark (per gad-81 + the agentskills.io methodology) where `delta.pass_rate > 0` is GRADUATED to canonical. (3) Emergent skills (those under `.agents/skills/emergent/` with `origin: emergent` frontmatter, authored by an agent rather than explicit human intent) STAY emergent forever â€” even if they graduate on the effectiveness axis, they keep the emergent label as a provenance signal. Graduation for emergent skills means "the skill is proven useful" not "the skill moves out of emergent." This matches the user's direction and also aligns with the CSH research framing: emergent skills are the compounding test variable and mixing them with human-authored canonical skills would contaminate the measurement. A `status: experimental | canonical` field is added to every SKILL.md frontmatter starting with this decision. Skills without the field default to experimental.

### Impact

(1) Every SKILL.md frontmatter gains a `status: experimental | canonical` field. All 27 current workspace skills start as experimental because none have evals/evals.json authored yet. (2) Prebuild scanCatalog() extracts the status field and displays it as a badge on the /skills index + detail pages. (3) The /skills index gains a new filter tab: "experimental" vs "canonical." New users looking at the catalog can filter for battle-tested skills. (4) Graduation is a reviewable action â€” once a skill's benchmark shows `delta.pass_rate > 0`, a decision entry documents the graduation and the frontmatter flips. (5) Emergent skills keep `origin: emergent` even after graduation. The two fields are orthogonal: origin tracks authorship lineage, status tracks evaluation evidence. (6) The SKEPTIC cross-cutting critique about "we call things hypotheses without evidence" gets an orthogonal fix for skills specifically: experimental vs canonical is the skill-level version of preliminary observation vs finding.

## gad-85: Adopt npx skills (vercel-labs/skills) as the canonical user-facing skill installer

### Summary

User directive 2026-04-09: "find skills can just reuse the npx skill.sh and gad can really just wrap around that making the calls underneath just so gad is like a barrel for tools a user would want and we also would need." vercel-labs/skills (`npx skills`) is the CLI for the open agent skills ecosystem â€” one command that supports 40+ coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Cline, OpenCode, Copilot, Continue, and more) and handles add/list/find/remove/update/check. It respects the `.agents/skills/` convention from gad-80 and works with GitHub shorthand (`owner/repo`), full URLs, and local paths. Policy: GAD does NOT reimplement skill installation. Users install GAD's workspace skills via `npx skills add MagicbornStudios/get-anything-done`. GAD's own `find-skills` skill becomes a thin methodology wrapper around `npx skills find`. The workspace-skill copy pass I added to `install.js` in task 22-46 remains in place as a fallback for users who prefer one-command install via `gad install`, but the canonical recommendation on /contribute + /standards is `npx skills`. The effect is that GAD becomes "a barrel for tools a user would want" â€” we aggregate skill research, methodology, hypothesis testing, and eval framework, and delegate skill installation to the open ecosystem tool that already solved the distribution problem.

### Impact

(1) The `find-skills` skill (new this turn per gad-73 triumvirate) is authored as a methodology wrapper around `npx skills find`. It does NOT implement its own search logic. (2) /contribute updated (queued) to tell users "install GAD skills via `npx skills add MagicbornStudios/get-anything-done`" as the first-class path. (3) /standards updated (queued) to cite vercel-labs/skills alongside the Anthropic PDF and agentskills.io. (4) The install.js workspace-skills copy (22-46) is retained but labelled as fallback â€” the canonical path is npx skills. (5) /security page gains a new mitigation: skills installed via npx skills inherit the ecosystem's trust posture (public repos, git provenance, hash-pinning). (6) Substantial simplification â€” GAD stops competing with the distribution tool and focuses on what it actually does (skill methodology + evaluation framework + hypothesis testing).

## gad-84: Hook-level introspection during evals â€” what "thoughts" can we actually capture?

### Summary

User question 2026-04-09: "what are some of the thoughts that claude or an agent is having that we can hook in to and analyze during evaluations." Concrete answer based on what Claude Code's hook system exposes today: (1) PreToolUse + PostToolUse hooks fire on every tool call with the tool name, arguments, and result. Captured by our trace v4 handler (gad-58, gad-50). This gives us observable ACTION, not thought. (2) Extended thinking blocks â€” when the Anthropic API emits `thinking` content blocks in a response, these appear in Claude Code's session.jsonl but are NOT exposed via hooks today. They are the closest thing to "agent thought" we can get. Currently we can access them POST-HOC by reading session.jsonl but cannot act on them during a run. (3) Message text between tool calls â€” the agent's natural-language output when explaining or planning. Captured in session.jsonl, also not hooked. (4) Permission/approval events â€” when the agent requests a permission, we see the request and the user's response. Captured via hooks. What we CAN do today: read session.jsonl after a run completes and extract the thinking blocks + message text as an offline enrichment of TRACE.json, annotate each thinking block with the tool uses that followed it, and use that as a signal for "agent reasoning quality" during a run. What we CANNOT do today: interrupt a run based on thinking content, steer it via the thinking stream, or measure thought quality in real time. The gap is Claude Code's hook surface, not the framework's.

### Impact

(1) New programmatic-eval gap captured in GAPS.md as G13: extract thinking blocks and message text from session.jsonl post-hoc and attach to TRACE.json under a new `derived.reasoning_trace` field. Offline enrichment â€” no new hooks required. Feasibility: high. Severity: medium (it's agent self-narrative which is suspect, but having it is better than not having it). (2) New open question: is there a programmatic signal we can extract from thinking blocks that correlates with run quality? Candidate: ratio of "exploration" thoughts to "execution" thoughts, thought-to-tool-use interval, thought length variance. Needs exploratory data analysis once we have the extraction pipeline. (3) Does NOT commit us to interrupting runs mid-flight based on thinking â€” that would require hooking the message stream, which Claude Code doesn't expose today.

## gad-83: find-skills scope â€” installed skills in user workspace + pointer to install sources, not a global crawl

### Summary

Per gad-73 the fundamental triumvirate is find-skills + merge-skill + create-skill. User clarification 2026-04-09: "our find-skills should probably just be for installed skills and giving us suitable options to merge or to point us to installing some skills from skill.sh or the gad cli and we just install from the gad github repo since its public." Concrete scope for find-skills (to be built): (1) scan the USER'S current workspace at `.agents/skills/` and `.claude/skills/` (the standard-conformant convention + Claude Code's native path, per gad-80), (2) scan the USER'S global skills at `~/.agents/skills/` and `~/.claude/skills/`, (3) return a ranked list of relevant skills for the current task based on description matching, (4) when no suitable skill is found, suggest candidate skills from external sources â€” skill.sh, the GAD github repo (github.com/MagicbornStudios/get-anything-done/tree/main/skills/), and Anthropic's official examples repo (github.com/anthropics/skills) â€” with install commands. find-skills does NOT: (a) crawl the network looking for skills unsupervised, (b) auto-install anything (the user approves), (c) return skills from external sources alongside local ones without clearly labeling origin. This keeps find-skills scoped to "what do I have access to RIGHT NOW, and what should I install if I want more." The triumvirate audit task (queued from gad-73) must now include building find-skills to this spec.

### Impact

(1) Task for triumvirate audit extended: find-skills spec is the scope defined above. create-skill and merge-skill specs still TBD. (2) When find-skills is built, it becomes the primary entry point for the skill-evolution loop. The agent runs find-skills first, then merge-skill if a suitable fundamental exists, then create-skill only if no merge candidate was found. (3) find-skills output format: structured JSON with `installed: [...]`, `suggested_from_external: [...]` with origin labels, so the user can see which are trusted (installed locally) vs unverified (external suggestions). (4) External-source integration with github.com/MagicbornStudios/get-anything-done is trivial â€” same-owner public repo, no auth needed. skill.sh and anthropics/skills integration is a follow-up.

## gad-82: Eval isolation â€” worktrees must not inherit parent monorepo skills, hooks, or settings

### Summary

User concern 2026-04-09: "a concern of mine when running the evals and rounds is that we might be polluting the experiments with our repo and monorepo installation." Valid concern. Eval runs currently live under `.claude/worktrees/agent-*/` spawned by `gad worktree`. Inside that worktree, the agent may inherit: (a) the parent repo's `.claude/settings.json` (including our trace hook handler absolute path), (b) workspace skills at `vendor/get-anything-done/skills/` (reachable via parent-directory scan or symlink), (c) GAD CLI tooling on PATH, (d) any environment variables set in the parent session. For the bare eval condition â€” which is supposed to test "no framework" â€” any of these leakages would contaminate the experiment by giving the bare agent implicit access to GAD affordances it shouldn't have. This decision commits us to eval isolation: (1) Eval worktrees run with an explicit-include list of files â€” the agent sees ONLY the eval project's requirements, the AGENTS.md for its workflow condition, and whatever skills the condition explicitly declares inherited. (2) The trace hook handler is ALLOWED (it's the instrumentation layer per gad-50 â€” not a framework affordance). (3) Everything else from the parent repo is either hidden, removed from PATH, or explicitly blocklisted. (4) An isolation audit runs before every round 5+ eval verifying no parent-repo state is reachable from inside the worktree.

### Impact

(1) New open question captured: does Claude Code actually honor `.claude/settings.json` from parent directories inside a worktree, or only the worktree's own? Needs concrete verification. (2) Task 22-48 queued: write an isolation audit script `scripts/audit-worktree-isolation.mjs` that walks a fresh worktree and reports every file + env var + path entry the agent could see. Run it before round 5. (3) SKEPTIC gains a new cross-cutting critique: "eval isolation is unverified â€” previous eval scores may be contaminated by parent-repo state." (4) Round 5 prerequisite: pass the isolation audit OR document exactly which parent-repo affordances are allowed + why. (5) New tasks under phase 22 capturing the investigation + fix.

## gad-81: Skill count policy â€” hard cap 3-7 active per task, uniqueness enforced, collision detection required

### Summary

Per the ChatGPT analysis the user shared (2026-04-09) and the agentskills.io progressive disclosure architecture, more skills does NOT equal more capability. The failure modes are: (1) context window saturation when many skills are injected or considered, (2) skill selection ambiguity when descriptions overlap, (3) latent prompt interference even when only one skill is used, (4) retrieval noise (for embedding-based lookup â€” not applicable to us yet). The agentskills.io progressive-disclosure model mitigates the biggest failure mode (saturation) by loading only the catalog at startup, but collision and ambiguity are still our responsibility. Policy committed: (A) Hard cap of 3-7 actively-loaded skills per task. The agent should select deliberately and explain why each is loaded. (B) Each skill must answer "what can this do that no other skill can?" â€” if the answer is unclear, the skill is a merge candidate. (C) Skill collision detection runs at prebuild: scan every SKILL.md description for overlapping trigger patterns, emit warnings, propose merge candidates. (D) Skill effectiveness is measured per the agentskills.io methodology: with_skill vs without_skill baseline runs per skill, stored in `evals/evals.json` inside each skill directory, aggregated into a `benchmark.json` per iteration. This is the per-skill equivalent of our eval-project rubric. (E) More skills = more pruning required. Use merge-skill (when it exists per gad-73) to fuse overlapping skills rather than accumulate them.

### Impact

(1) New task queued: scan `skills/*/SKILL.md` descriptions for overlapping trigger keywords, emit a collision report. (2) New task queued: adopt the agentskills.io eval format for per-skill evaluation â€” author `evals/evals.json` templates for the fundamental triumvirate skills once they exist. (3) The agent-authored evaluation framework for the escape-the-dungeon family remains the rubric-based GAD eval; the per-skill evaluation is the agentskills.io methodology. These are complementary, not competing. (4) Skills directory site page gains a "collision candidates" section if any are detected. (5) Data provenance surface on /data gains a new source: "skill-collision-scan".

## gad-80: Adopt agentskills.io standard â€” .agents/skills/ convention + cross-client interoperability

### Summary

The Agent Skills standard at agentskills.io defines a client-agnostic format and discovery convention for skills: every skill is a folder containing a SKILL.md with YAML frontmatter (name + description required) and a markdown body. Progressive disclosure is a three-tier load strategy â€” catalog (name+description only, ~50-100 tokens/skill, loaded at session start), instructions (full SKILL.md body, loaded on activation), resources (scripts/references/assets, on-demand). The standard's discovery convention for cross-client interoperability is `&lt;project&gt;/.agents/skills/` and `~/.agents/skills/` â€” widely adopted so skills installed by one compliant client become visible to any other. GAD currently uses `skills/` at the repo root, which is neither the Claude-Code native path (`.claude/skills/`) nor the cross-client convention (`.agents/skills/`). This decision adopts agentskills.io as a co-equal canonical reference alongside the Anthropic skills guide (gad-70), commits us to the `.agents/skills/` convention for installable skills, and mandates citation of both sources on every skill-related page on the site. Does NOT replace our existing `skills/` workspace directory â€” workspace skills remain at that location for in-repo use â€” but new installable skills go to `.agents/skills/` and `install.js` is extended to copy both when supported by the target runtime.

### Impact

(1) MIGRATION COMPLETE 2026-04-09: moved `skills/` â†’ `.agents/skills/` via git mv preserving all 28 skills (27 human-authored + 1 emergent). Prebuild SKILLS_DIR updated to read from the new canonical path. Site page file references bumped to `.agents/skills/...`. (2) New `.planning/docs/SKILL-FINDABILITY-2026-04-09.md` captures the full findability investigation. (3) The SKEPTIC cross-cutting critique about "one-person research framework" gains an orthogonal defense: we align with an open standard, so our skill format is auditable against external convention. (4) STILL PENDING on task 22-46: install.js must be extended to copy `.agents/skills/` contents (minus emergent) to user workspaces when `gad install` runs. (5) Task 22-47 queued: cite agentskills.io + Anthropic PDF on every skill-related site page via the new /standards page. (6) Emergent skill lives at `.agents/skills/emergent/session-discipline/SKILL.md` â€” the subdirectory structure is the signal that marks it emergent, mirrored by the `origin: emergent` frontmatter flag.

## gad-79: task_pressure operationalized â€” programmatic, structural, Shannon-inspired

### Summary

Decision gad-75 introduced "pressure" as a first-class dimension but left the formula open. User answered the open question on 2026-04-09: pressure is computed PROGRAMMATICALLY from REQUIREMENTS.xml STRUCTURE, with no human override, no `<pressure-profile>` block, and no subjective scoring (ambiguity, failure cost, etc. are dropped as sub-dimensions). The formulation borrows the Shannon-entropy intuition without computing entropy directly: "pressure â‰ˆ the number of decisions/questions required to satisfy the system." Each requirement is a question, each gate is a resolved state, more requirements and more cross-cuts mean more decisions. Concrete inputs: (a) R = count of `&lt;requirement&gt;` elements (including inside `&lt;addendum&gt;`), (b) G = count of `&lt;gate&gt;` elements, each weighted 2x because a gate groups multiple decisions, (c) C = count of `amends` attribute values across requirements (cross-cut dependencies), (d) W = word-count proxy for raw scope. Formula: `task_pressure_raw = R + 2*G + C` and `task_pressure_score = log2(task_pressure_raw + 1) / log2(MAX_EXPECTED + 1)` normalized to 0-1. Stored in TRACE.json per run (not a rubric dimension â€” Q2 answered A, metadata only). Critical naming clarification: this is `task_pressure` (structural, programmatic) and must NOT be conflated with `game_pressure` (the player-experience concept of stamina / resource exhaustion / time pressure in the escape-the-dungeon game). They are separate systems. The UI may display either as "pressure" but the internal names stay distinct.

### Impact

(1) Prebuild gains a `computeTaskPressure(xml)` function called once per REQUIREMENTS.xml version, emitting per-version scores to `TASK_PRESSURE` in eval-data.generated.ts. (2) /roadmap's `PRESSURE_BY_ROUND` hand-typed constant is replaced by computed values, with a data-provenance note pointing at the formula. (3) Per-run pages gain a `task_pressure` badge showing the requirements-version pressure that run was operating under. (4) Cross-round comparison pages can now normalize: "agent X scored 0.8 at task_pressure 0.42." (5) gad-75 supersedes: the 5-subdimension framing is replaced by the structural 3-input formula (R/G/C with W as a secondary proxy). gad-75's conceptual framing remains valid as motivation; gad-79 is the operational definition. (6) The formula is intentionally simple and transparent so it can be audited and disputed. Open question pressure-score-formula is RESOLVED by this decision. Open question pressure-as-rubric-vs-metadata is RESOLVED as metadata-only. (7) game_pressure remains as the in-game design concept referenced in REQUIREMENTS addendum R-v5 items and the /requirements page rendering; no overlap with task_pressure.

## gad-78: Dogfood failure observed â€” session 2026-04-09 built GAD without using GAD

### Summary

During the 2026-04-09 IA refactor session, Claude Code (the agent building the project) shipped 20+ surfaces (pages, components, prebuild extensions, decisions) without following GAD's own workflow. Specifically: (1) did not run `gad snapshot` between turns â€” ran it once early in the session and never rehydrated, (2) did not check TASK-REGISTRY.xml for matching planned tasks despite 22-20 /gad page / 22-21 /methodology / 22-23 /projects / 22-24 interactive charts / 22-25 nav update / 22-31 Remotion scaffolding all being PLANNED in the registry for exactly the work that was then done ad-hoc, (3) did not invoke a single one of the 26 skills shipping in `skills/`, most notably `portfolio-sync` whose entire purpose is the workflow the agent executed manually, (4) did not spawn any subagents (gad-planner, gad-phase-researcher, gad-codebase-mapper all existed and were relevant), (5) wrote SKEPTIC.md into `.planning/` without checking whether that location is in `gad snapshot`'s output surface â€” it is not, so the doc is invisible to future agent sessions unless a human points at it, (6) used Claude Code's session-scoped TaskCreate tool instead of TASK-REGISTRY.xml, creating 40+ ephemeral tasks that vanish when the session ends. This is the most meta failure possible: the agent testing whether framework discipline helps agents was not using the framework. Full writeup in `.planning/SESSION-REFLECTION-2026-04-09.md`. THIS IS A FINDING AGAINST THE COMPOUND-SKILLS HYPOTHESIS as currently framed: skills do not compound if the agent doesn't invoke them, and nothing in the framework currently forces invocation.

### Impact

Immediate fixes this session: (1) new `skills/session-discipline/SKILL.md` codifying the hard-start checklist every agent turn must follow (snapshot â†’ check registry â†’ scan skills â†’ adopt or create task â†’ work â†’ write skills for non-obvious patterns â†’ update registry â†’ update STATE â†’ commit with task id â†’ push), (2) SKEPTIC.md gets a new dogfood-failure section, (3) new task queued: extend `gad snapshot` to list `.planning/*.md` ad-hoc docs so future sessions see ASSUMPTIONS/GAPS/SKEPTIC/SESSION-REFLECTION automatically, (4) new task queued: retroactively update TASK-REGISTRY.xml marking 22-20/22-21/22-23/22-24/22-25 as done where the work actually shipped. Longer-term: the framework needs self-enforcement â€” hooks at session boundaries, skill-trigger coverage checks (GAPS G2) that fail when a tool-use pattern matches a skill description but no skill invocation event fires, commit-message linting that rejects commits without a task id. Until that happens, agents will pattern-match around the framework and the research data we collect will be contaminated by "agent did not actually use framework."

## gad-77: Contribution flow is human-first â€” snapshot is agent-facing, not human-facing

### Summary

Important clarification the user surfaced during the IA planning conversation: `gad snapshot` is a command for AGENTS to rehydrate context after compaction â€” it produces no useful output for a human reading it. A human contribution flow must NOT include running snapshot as a step. The correct human flow is: (1) clone the repo, (2) npm install (or pnpm/yarn), (3) open the repo in Claude Code (or Cursor/Codex/other agent runtime), (4) the GAD skills are already installed in the repo so the agent has them available, (5) the human talks conversationally to the agent about running an eval or introducing a new hypothesis. The human never reads STATE.xml; they read the site's /findings, /decisions, /questions pages. The agent reads STATE.xml on their behalf when they ask it to. **Contribution pages must explicitly separate the human path from the agent path** so users don't follow agent-facing instructions and get confused by the output. Decision also commits us to TESTING the contribution flow on a fresh clone in a new repo before publishing the /contribute page, to verify skills are actually present and usable without manual setup steps we've forgotten to document.

### Impact

(1) /contribute page ships with two clearly separated sections: "Human workflow" and "What the agent does behind the scenes". (2) The human workflow is bare bones: clone â†’ install â†’ open in Claude â†’ talk. No snapshot, no XML editing, no planning loop explanation. (3) The agent workflow section is a short explainer so humans know the difference but aren't expected to follow it. (4) Before shipping /contribute, we run a fresh-clone test in a new repo to verify: (a) skills copy into .claude/, (b) the agent can snapshot on first run, (c) a conversational request like "run the escape-the-dungeon-bare eval" actually works. (5) No PR template, no eval submission rubric, no contribution guidelines doc yet â€” those are process overhead we don't need until we have contributors. (6) CONTRIBUTING.md in the repo root is queued as a follow-up after the fresh-clone test succeeds.

## gad-76: Target users + value-prop framing â€” researchers primary, indie devs experiential, pressure as the hook

### Summary

Formal statement of who the site is for and what it says. **Primary user: coding-agent researchers** (target A) â€” people evaluating skill frameworks, hypothesis quality, and measurement rigor. They read findings, click through to DECISIONS.xml on GitHub, scrutinize the rubric construction. They tolerate dense content and reject marketing copy. **Secondary user: indie devs curious about skill evolution** (target C) â€” they land via playable demos, stay for the research transparency, need jargon wrapped in tooltips. **Not the audience (yet):** enterprise teams evaluating for procurement, framework authors looking to fork GAD wholesale. **Value proposition working line:** *"A system for evaluating and evolving agents through real tasks, measurable pressure, and iteration."* This combines three load-bearing ideas: evaluating agents on real tasks (not benchmarks), measurable pressure (gad-75), and iteration (compound-skills hypothesis). **Explicitly NOT the value prop:** "Use GAD to ship software faster." The freedom hypothesis (gad-36) shows bare agents outperform framework agents on greenfield creative implementation â€” GAD's value is the measurement layer and task-management substrate, not shipping speed.

### Impact

(1) Landing page rewrite lands this session: hero headline uses the pressure framing, primary CTA is Play (B), above-the-fold priority stack is Play â†’ Methodology â†’ Findings â†’ Hypothesis â†’ Fork. (2) Copy across all pages must assume primary user A (no oversimplifying, no marketing), but must wrap jargon in Term tooltips for secondary user C. (3) Everything we build must serve user A or user C â€” if it serves neither, it's out of scope for now. (4) The open question gad-value-prop-landing-page-framing is RESOLVED by this decision + the landing rewrite that ships alongside it. (5) ASSUMPTIONS.md captures full details of both users + what we're NOT building for them.

## gad-75: Pressure is a first-class eval dimension â€” the measurable axis connecting game-feel, requirements, and skill evolution

### Summary

Introduced during the IA planning conversation on 2026-04-09: "Pressure" becomes a first-class evaluation dimension alongside quality, iteration count, and composite score. Working definition: pressure = the constraint intensity applied to the agent during an eval, composed of five sub-dimensions â€” (1) requirement complexity (moving parts, cross-system interactions, logic depth), (2) ambiguity (clarity, missing info, interpretation required), (3) constraint density (hard rules, architecture constraints, stack lock-in), (4) iteration budget (tool-use ceiling, retry passes, rate/529 limits), (5) failure cost (gate vs scored, visible vs hidden, cascading vs isolated). Pressure is what the requirements have been implicitly encoding, what the game design has been trying to deliver to players, and what the eval framework has been partially measuring without naming. Requirement versions implicitly ARE pressure tiers â€” v1 was low pressure, v5 is high pressure, and rounds ARE pressure transitions. Promoting pressure to a named variable lets us normalize cross-round comparisons and ask "how did agent X perform under pressure tier Y" instead of just "what score did X get". This is distinct from the rubric (which measures result quality) â€” pressure is metadata about the test conditions. Pressure ALSO connects the game-feel thread (players feel pressure as challenge, ingenuity is pressure on the player) to the eval-rigor thread, which is the project's strongest differentiator. The landing page value-prop reframes around "evaluating and evolving agents through real tasks, measurable pressure, and iteration" (gad-76).

### Impact

(1) Pressure score formula is a new research task â€” open question opened in data/open-questions.json. (2) REQUIREMENTS.xml templates gain an optional `<pressure-profile>` block per version capturing the five sub-dimensions as author-rated scores; this is self-rated metadata initially, refined later with programmatic extraction. (3) Per-run page gains a pressure tier badge next to the composite score. (4) /roadmap timeline renders pressure progression across rounds as a new axis. (5) A future /pressure page (queued, not yet built) will let users see agent-performance-vs-pressure as a scatter. (6) Findings writeups must name the pressure tier they're evaluating under. (7) This decision is the biggest single-session conceptual shift the project has made; it reframes the landing page entirely and becomes a major signal of research credibility versus "just another agent framework" positioning.

## gad-74: GAD's actual value proposition â€” task management at scale in-repo + skill evaluation, NOT "ship software faster"

### Summary

User articulation: "the main value I am seeing with GAD is not software implementation fully as products. but definitely a good task management at scale all while in repo. get-anything-done's repo and the site/data is all in repo and we encourage others to clone and contribute any experiments and builds they want." Formal statement of GAD's value proposition, anchored by months of eval data: (1) **Durable task management at scale with everything in-repo.** `.planning/` XML + `data/` JSON + `evals/` artifacts + `site/` generated TS â€” no external database, no external service, no SaaS lock-in. Fork the repo, run `gad snapshot`, contribute. (2) **Scientific framework for proving skill effectiveness and workflow discipline.** The eval framework is the load-bearing feature: it tests whether skills, frameworks, and workflows actually produce better work using structured experiments (rounds, rubrics, gates, derived metrics). (3) **Skill security posture** (longer-term ambition per gad-73) â€” trust model, provenance, evaluation of effectiveness. Explicitly NOT GAD's primary value: "use GAD to ship your production software faster." The freedom hypothesis (gad-36) already showed bare agents can outperform on creative implementation. GAD may produce disciplined garbage for greenfield game dev while being excellent for task-heavy coordination, planning, and evaluation work. The site's framing must reflect this honestly.

### Impact

Landing page rewrite (queued): hero copy stops implying "use GAD to build things" and leads with "understand whether agents are actually building things well, and keep everything in the repo." Open question gad-value-prop-landing-page-framing captures the specific rewrite pass. /methodology page must reference this decision so first-time visitors understand the research framing. Future eval categories (brownfield, content-pack injection per gad-66, codex-vs-claude comparison per task 89) all serve the value-prop of scientific skill/workflow evaluation, not of software shipping speed.

## gad-73: Fundamental skills triumvirate â€” create-skill / merge-skill / find-skills as GAD's foundational set

### Summary

User vision: GAD provides three fundamental skills that together enable the emergent-evolution hypothesis (gad-68) to work in practice. (1) **find-skills** â€” locate a trusted fundamental skill from the GAD ecosystem. Examples of fundamentals: scientific-method, debug, write-tests, profile-performance, investigate-failure. Inspired by https://skills.sh/vercel-labs/skills/find-skills but scoped to the GAD trusted pool initially (expanding later). (2) **merge-skill** â€” fuse a fundamental into a more project-attuned skill. Example: merge scientific-method into kaplay-rune-spellcrafting-debug, producing scientific-method-for-kaplay-rune-spellcrafting â€” a project-tailored skill with the fundamental's rigor baked in. (3) **create-skill** â€” author genuinely new skills when no merge candidate exists. This triumvirate is the META LAYER of the in-game rune/spell merging mechanic the evals are producing: the agent's skill library evolves via the same combine-fundamentals pattern the game teaches players. This is not coincidence â€” it's the most visible instance of the craftsman metaphor (gad-68) working at two levels simultaneously. Goal: build up a library of skills with fundamental expert practices tailored in, across many greenfield prototypes + brownfield applications. Security posture: the bigger vision is to "provide security for skills, prove skills effectiveness and workflows with builds" â€” a trust model where merging a fundamental into a tailored skill carries the fundamental's provenance forward.

### Impact

Audit task queued (task 103 follow-up): walk skills/ and identify which of the triumvirate already exist. create-skill likely exists. merge-skill and find-skills may not. Build whichever is missing. Mark the triumvirate as `category: foundational` in frontmatter so they're visually distinguished on the site's skills directory (task 85). The emergent eval template must bootstrap-inherit all three fundamentals so every emergent run has the tools to find+merge+create from day one. This also answers the CSH hygiene question from gad-65: a healthy emergent run should show evidence of find-skills lookups, merge-skill invocations authoring tailored variants, and create-skill usage only when merging failed. Tracked via hook events per GAPS G2 and G11.

## gad-72: Rounds framework â€” requirements-versioned, progressive complexity, hypothesis revisits allowed

### Summary

Formal statement of the rounds framework the project has been operating under informally. (1) A round is DEFINED by a requirements-document version. New requirements version = new round. This is the unit of "what are we testing against." (2) Multiple eval versions can ship inside a single round â€” round 4 has etd v9, etd v10, bare v5, emergent v4 (4 builds across 3 projects) and all belong to round 4 because they all target requirements v4. Within-round versions exist because different agents and workflows produce different builds against the same requirements, and because we retry runs that were rate-limited or api-interrupted. (3) Rounds are progressively MORE complex â€” requirements expand monotonically from v1â†’v2â†’...â†’v5. Each round's requirements include or supersede the previous. No requirements are silently dropped; deferrals are explicit in the version history. (4) Earlier rounds CAN be re-evaluated with NEW hypotheses. Not every build in an earlier round can be trusted for every hypothesis â€” the measurement frameworks, metrics, and harnesses at round 2 were weaker than at round 4. But earlier rounds still produce data useful for hypotheses that weren't the primary test at the time. (5) A new hypothesis can START a new round against the current requirements version OR an earlier one. Example: "does codex-cli build similarly to claude-code given the same v4 requirements?" is a valid new round-against-v4 producing a new line of data without requiring a v6. (6) COMPLEXITY LADDER as a research tool: different requirements versions represent different complexity levels; a hypothesis might only be answerable at a specific level. A runtime comparison (claude vs codex) might work best at v3 where the requirements are simpler and the comparison is cleaner.

### Impact

Site must surface: (a) per-eval-project list of rounds the project has participated in with which requirements version was targeted, (b) per-round list of every eval version that ran, (c) per-run display of both the requirements version targeted AND a link to the previous round's requirements for context. REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md is the authoritative ladder. New eval runs MUST pick a requirements_version that exists in REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md; if a new version is needed the version must be added before the run. The `/roadmap` timeline page (task 88) renders this framework as the narrative of the project â€” what requirements version was tested when, what shipped, what got deferred. Earlier-round revisits (e.g. running codex against v3) are first-class and must not be labelled as "outdated" â€” they're new data points with different complexity.

## gad-71: data/ JSON pseudo-database for hand-curated site content

### Summary

User proposal: "we might want to have a pseudo database that is json some json that gets stored in the repo and we use that to collect all the static data we need for the site and operate like it is a database, just in repo as dont expect customers and sensitive information at all." Formalize the pattern: `vendor/get-anything-done/data/*.json` files are the authoritative source of hand-curated content for the site. The prebuild script (`site/scripts/build-site-data.mjs`) reads them at build time and emits typed TypeScript via `site/lib/*.generated.ts`. The site never runs queries at request time â€” every page is SSG, every data file is read once at prebuild, types are statically exported. This is distinct from `.planning/` XML (which is agent-authored process state like phase/task/decision) and from `evals/<project>/<version>/TRACE.json` (which is eval-run output). `data/` is specifically for curated knowledge that doesn't belong in either of those two places: open questions for the public, observed bugs across eval runs, glossary terms, user-workflow assumptions, content packs extracted for gad-66, human-review tracking metadata, etc. Security posture is explicit: this is a static site with no customers, no authentication, no PII, no secrets. JSON-in-git is appropriate because we have no need for ACID, transactions, or concurrent writers â€” one human edits files in a branch, the prebuild regenerates, the site ships. If we ever need any of those properties (we don't expect to), the migration path is clear: JSON schemas become table definitions, the prebuild becomes a seed script.

### Impact

(1) Create `vendor/get-anything-done/data/` with an initial set of files: `open-questions.json`, `bugs.json`, `glossary.json`, `assumptions.json`. (2) Extend `build-site-data.mjs` with a new section that reads each data file, validates shape, and writes corresponding TS exports. (3) New site routes backed by these files: `/questions` (open-questions.json), eventually `/glossary`, `/assumptions`. (4) Every file gets a top-level `_schema` field documenting its shape + a `_last_updated` field maintained by hand. (5) When a new hand-curated data type is needed, the pattern is: add `data/&lt;name&gt;.json` â†’ add a reader to `build-site-data.mjs` â†’ add a typed export to `lib/&lt;name&gt;.generated.ts` â†’ build a consuming page. No new runtime services, no servers, no API routes.

## gad-70: Anthropic skills guide is the canonical reference for skill quality

### Summary

The Anthropic "Complete Guide to Building Skills for Claude" (resources.anthropic.com/hubfs/The-Complete-Guide-to-Building-Skill-for-Claude.pdf) is adopted as the canonical reference for what a good skill looks like and how to test one. Three testing layers from the guide are named: (1) triggering tests â€” does the skill load on relevant / paraphrased / negative queries, (2) functional tests â€” valid outputs, API calls succeed, error handling works, edge cases covered, (3) performance comparison â€” proves the skill improves results vs. baseline using defined success criteria. Three skill categories from the guide are adopted as metadata on our authored skills: doc/asset creation, workflow automation, MCP enhancement. Iteration signals (undertriggering, overtriggering, execution issues) become named failure modes in our skill failure-mode taxonomy. Every skill we author or evolve must be auditable against this guide, and /methodology + /rubric + a new /skills-guide page must cite the PDF so users can follow our lineage to Anthropic's own guidance.

### Impact

(1) skills/create-skill.md body must reference the guide and add a "category" frontmatter field (doc-creation|workflow|mcp-enhancement). (2) A /skills-guide route on the site embeds the three testing layers with examples and links to the PDF. (3) Every /skills/[id] detail page shows which of the three categories the skill belongs to and whether it has triggering/functional/performance-comparison tests. (4) The emergent eval's skill_inheritance_effectiveness rubric dimension is extended to check whether newly-authored skills follow the guide's description/trigger format. (5) Continual review cadence: each round's findings writeup must answer "did we drift from the Anthropic guide since the last round?"

## gad-69: Programmatic evaluation is the priority â€” minimize agent-self-report dependency

### Summary

User directive: "I don't want to be too dependent on the agents following guides/tasks to evaluate agents following guides/tasks." Evaluation framework must prioritize objective data produced by tooling, hooks, tracing, servers, parsing, and automation over agent self-reports, human judgment, or "did the agent follow the rubric" checks. Every new eval metric must answer "can this be collected programmatically?" before "how do we score it?" â€” and if programmatic collection is feasible, it takes priority. Human review stays for quality dimensions that genuinely need human judgment (is the UI enjoyable, does the game feel right) but everything mechanically checkable (did the skill actually load, did the gate pass, did the tool use mix look right, did the agent merge skills when it should have) must be captured automatically. This is the strongest framing of gad-61 to date.

### Impact

New ongoing research task: audit every current eval data field for "programmatic vs human-reported" and build a gaps list. Candidates to automate immediately: skill-triggering coverage (did the skill load on the right tool_use patterns), functional gate checks (playwright smoke tests for G1-G4), tool-use discipline signals (over/under-triggering relative to skill descriptions), sub-agent spawn counts, commit rhythm, plan-adherence delta, deviation count, build+test pass/fail. Deliverable: `.planning/GAPS.md` with each gap ranked by eval impact and which phase picks it up. Every new eval metric PR must include a line stating whether the metric is programmatic or human-reported.

## gad-68: Emergent-evolution working hypothesis â€” skill-evolution + emergent projects + tailored merging

### Summary

Synthesis of the freedom hypothesis (gad-36), compound-skills hypothesis (gad-65), and the consistent GAD-framework hiccups observed across rounds 1-4 into a single working hypothesis. Statement: "A coding agent given blank planning artifacts, requirements, a task, and the ability to (a) create new skills, (b) merge similar skills, (c) find related skills to help, plus GAD-provided foundational skills to merge against, will produce better work than framework-constrained or bare conditions over time â€” because projects are themselves emergent, and skills must be tailored to the specific project continually and merged with similar foundational skills to maintain consistency." The craftsman/lifter metaphor: a master lifter varies exercise and lowers weight to stay sharp, the craftsman returns to fundamentals to maintain form â€” depending on the goal, the agent should tailor to the project while rehearsing fundamentals. This aligns with what the original Ralph Wiggum loop creator and the repomirror creators have written about as well. The GAD framework's role is NOT to prescribe process â€” it's to provide the foundational skill pool to merge against and an evolution substrate for project-tailored skills to form on top. This hypothesis is the research goal of the emergent eval condition; freedom hypothesis tests bare vs framework on a single round while this hypothesis tests whether GAD-with-evolution compounds over many rounds into project mastery. Distinct from vanilla compound-skills: this adds the merging + foundational-rehearsal dimensions and explicitly names GAD's role as a skill pool rather than a process prescription.

### Impact

Round 5+ emergent eval condition must include: (1) a foundational skill pool inherited from GAD that the agent can merge against, (2) a skill-merge tool the agent can invoke (e.g. `gad skill merge <a> <b>`), (3) a way to mark a skill as "project-tailored" vs "foundational rehearsal" in frontmatter, (4) the Nyquist audit for each round checks whether merging happened and whether project-tailored skills compound. Site must carry this framing on the landing page and /gad route as the first-class hypothesis, replacing or supplementing the freedom-hypothesis framing. Also: projects themselves are emergent â€” acknowledge that requirements evolve as we observe what the evolution produces (v5 addendum was born from playtest, not upfront design).

## gad-67: Serial eval execution is the permanent default â€” parallel requires explicit authorization

### Summary

Reinforcement of gad-62 with a stronger default. User directive after round 4: "our tests done sequentially to use the full token/budget that we get for these evals is more than likely what we will need to do rather than run them all in parallel most of the time. we will just need to remember to eval." Policy: any agent session proposing an eval run defaults to serial execution (one condition at a time, to completion, allowing the full per-session token budget to absorb any single run). Parallel execution is an explicit exception requiring (a) a stated reason why serial is insufficient, (b) a pre-computed token budget showing parallel runs will not collide on the shared account cap, and (c) explicit user authorization in the current session. Agents MUST NOT batch-launch parallel eval worktrees as a default. "We will just need to remember to eval" is also captured: agent sessions working on eval-relevant code must proactively surface "run the eval to validate?" after significant requirements or template changes.

### Impact

Update any documentation that implies parallel is the default (gad:eval-run skill, AGENTS.md for evals, portfolio site eval docs). `gad eval suite` docstring already clarifies this but should gain a top-of-output warning when invoked without the --parallel flag. Agents pick one eval at a time and run it to honest completion before queuing the next.

## gad-66: Authored-content injection experiment â€” inherit content packs alongside requirements

### Summary

New eval condition proposal: preserve every run's authored game content (spells, runes, items, NPCs, dialogue trees, skills, encounter tables) as a portable "content pack" separate from the source code. A subsequent eval run inherits both the requirements document AND a content pack extracted from prior runs, then builds on top. This is NOT a freedom-hypothesis test â€” it explicitly scaffolds the agent with pre-authored content and measures how much more fleshed out the resulting game becomes given the same requirements. User's framing: "given some requirements and some already made content, it will build on top of that. this will just possibly make a more fleshed out game." Terminal research objective (user verbatim): "have a really complex game made with all the builds and data around it for coding agent transparency and ability."
